issue_title: RuntimeError: Mixer (app.Model): Cannot generate a unique value for user
issue_body:
I'm getting this error in a large Django codebase with hundreds of tests. I believe this happens because all instances that mixer creates are somehow cached and after a while it runs out of dictionary words to create new instances because it compares against the cache and not against the DB (which is obviously empty after each test).

I kind of pinned it down to [this loop in main.py](https://github.com/klen/mixer/blob/develop/mixer/main.py#L256) and the fact that `TypeMixer` derives from `TypeMixerMeta` which has "Cache typemixers by scheme." as a docstring makes me think that these instances might act like singletons and are not properly destroyed between tests, so that `self.__gen_values` will probably just fill up more and more until the dictionary cannot produce unique items any more.

Is there any workaround for this?

issue_title: asserting JSON array contents in arbitrary order
issue_body:
It's not unusual for JSON APIs to return arrays whose order is irrelevant (i.e effectively sets, e.g. [JSON API](http://jsonapi.org/format/)'s `data` object graph).

Testing for entries within such arrays seems tricky. In order to assert that a specific entry exists, I'm currently resorting to this:

```yaml
response_json_paths: # XXX: order currently hard-coded, but not actually relevant
    $.data[0].id: login
    $.data[0].links.self: http://example.org/login
    $.data[1].id: profiles
    $.data[1].links.self: http://example.org/profiles
    $.data[2].id: posts
    $.data[2].links.self: http://example.org/posts
```

This is brittle because order isn't guaranteed. The only alternative seems to be to use filter expressions:

```yaml
$.data[?(@.attributes.id = "login")]
```

That's not particularly readable though.

Is this something #86 could help with, perhaps via `sorted`?

When running buildout with this requirement I get SyntaxErrors:

    yield from ...

May be this could be converted to:

    for bar in foo:
        yield bar

?

I think the empty byte string `b''` should encode to the empty string `""`. Instead, it currently encodes to `"11"`.

Similarly, the string `"1"` should decode to the byte `b'\x00'`. Instead, it currently decodes to `b'\x00\x00'`.

I'm not totally sure about the right behavior for Base58 padding, but I think at least it should be possible to round-trip any string of bytes. Right now if you send any number of zero-bytes (including the empty bytestring) through encode+decode, you get a different number of bytes back out.

issue_title: Muffin handlers hang when using a Rethinkdb connecton that was made outside of Muffin's loop
issue_body:
For some reason Muffin handlers hang when using a Rethinkdb connecton that was made outside of Muffin's loop. I realise that it isn't necessarily Muffin's fault, but please take a look at the very simple examples below.

**The following script works as expected** (returns "1+1 = 2" on each page load):

```python
import muffin
import rethinkdb as r

r.set_loop_type('asyncio')

app = muffin.Application('example')


@app.register('/')
async def handle(request):
	db = await r.connect()  # <--- Connect every time
	js = '1+1'
	res = await r.js(js).run(db)
	text = "%s = %s" % (js, res)
	return text


if __name__ == '__main__':
	app.manage()
```

**The following script hangs on page load** (and then it dies in long agony on ^C rather than simply quits):

```python
import muffin
import asyncio
import rethinkdb as r

r.set_loop_type('asyncio')

db = asyncio.get_event_loop().run_until_complete(r.connect())  # <--- Connect only once

app = muffin.Application('example')


@app.register('/')
async def handle(request):
	js = '1+1'
	res = await r.js(js).run(db)
	text = "%s = %s" % (js, res)
	return text


if __name__ == '__main__':
	app.manage()
```

I don't think it's purely Rethinkdb problem because **the same approach works with `aiohttp`**:

```python
from aiohttp import web
import asyncio
import rethinkdb as r

r.set_loop_type('asyncio')

db = asyncio.get_event_loop().run_until_complete(r.connect())  # <--- Connect only once


async def handle(request):
	js = '1+1'
	res = await r.js(js).run(db)
	text = "%s = %s" % (js, res)
	return web.Response(body=text.encode('utf-8'))


app = web.Application()
app.router.add_route('GET', '/', handle)

web.run_app(app)
```

Sometimes I use pint to get simple user input (e.g. strings) into appropriately parsed magnitudes and units.  It's actually quite good at this task even though it is not using the package to it's fullest and is certainly easier than rolling a custom parser when it already has such a good implementation.  My issue is that when parsing an input that may or may not have a unit attached, it causes downstream code protection (e.g. try...except clauses) due to the fact that a string without a unit is parsed as a float type and not an instance of the Quantity class.  Here's sample code to illustrate:

```python
import pint
print(pint.__version__) # 0.7.2
ureg = pint.UnitRegistry()

qty_str = '123.456 dimensionless'
qty = ureg.parse_expression(qty_str)
print(type(qty))  # <class 'pint.unit.Quantity'>
print(qty.magnitude) # 123.456
print(type(qty.magnitude)) # <type 'float'>

qty_str = '123.456'
qty = ureg.parse_expression(qty_str)
print(type(qty)) # <type 'float'>
print(qty.magnitude) # AttributeError: 'float' object has no attribute 'magnitude'
print(type(qty.magnitude)) # ...
```

Since when using `parse_expression` on a string we are explicitly asking pint to take our input and match it a Quantity object, the default result of a float for a unitless quantity is not what I would expect.  While this behavior may be convenient for interactive coding, it doesn't lead to programatically similar results even though it executes without error/warning.

What does everyone else think about this?  Is it a design flaw or a design feature?  I always get the 2 confused :wink:.

When executing certain conversions, it's possible to get results that differ between subsequent Python sessions. The differences are a result of floating point math being non-associative. If unit conversion factors are multiplied in a different order, small floating point errors can change direction. An example of this phenomenon is shown below:
```python
In [1]: 200 * 1e-3 / 1e-9
Out[1]: 200000000.0

In [2]: 1 / 1e-9 * 200 * 1e-3
Out[2]: 199999999.99999997
```
Pint essentially stores units in a dictionary (the UnitContainer object), and since the keys in such a dictionary are unordered, the order in which they will be multiplied during a conversion is non-deterministic. The following short script will exhibit different results as they are run in subsequent Python sessions.
```python
# pint_bug.py
from pint import UnitRegistry
units = UnitRegistry()

u1 = units.carat
u2 = units.ng
print([k for k in (u1._units / u2._units)])
print((1 * u1).to(u2).magnitude)
```
Running the test script multiple times
```bash
$ python pint_bug.py
['carat', 'nanogram']
200000000.0
$ python pint_bug.py
['nanogram', 'carat']
199999999.99999997
```
While there may not be a good solution for eliminating this floating point math error, I think we should at least make the unit conversion order deterministic to invoke the least surprise (and pain) upon users.

Do not write fusesoc.log by default
issue_body:
Fusesoc by default writes a file 'fusesoc.log' into the working directory. Even though this is common for many EDA tools, I suggest removing this functionality by default.

Why?
- Having two fusesoc runs in the same directory results in an "interleaved" fusesoc.log file, essentially a useless one.
- It's very uncommon behavior for any "non-EDA" tool.

Two ideas how to resolve this:
- Write the fusesoc.log file to the build directory (`build/DESIGNNAME/...`)
- Don't write a fusesoc.log file unless instructed so by e.g. a command line option (e.g. `--logfile=fusesoc.log`)

issue_title: Issues with LDAP3 Abstraction Layer while writing boolean values.
issue_body:
I'm currently using in my software the version 2.1.1 of ldap3.

In this software I'm interfacing with the ldap server through ldap3 and its pythonic Abstraction Layer. So far so good (it made the editing so fast), until I introduced an object with a boolean attribute. In fact I get the following bug on the logic of the Abstraction Layer:
 - when reading the value is return as boolean (`<type 'bool'>`) like the following:

```
DN: o=test,dc=example,dc=tld - STATUS: Writable, Pending changes - READ TIME: 2016-12-27T23:42:20.660217
    objectClass: testClass
                 top
    myBoolAttribute: True
```

 - when writing if I try to do a change inserting a pythonic boolean value (like the following) I get an _LDAPInvalidAttributeSyntaxResult_:

```
DN: o=test,dc=example,dc=tld - STATUS: Writable, Pending changes - READ TIME: 2016-12-27T23:42:20.660217
    objectClass: testClass
                 top
    myBoolAttribute: True
              CHANGES: [('MODIFY_REPLACE', [False])]
```
 * instead it works if I use, in setting the boolean value, a string like 'FALSE' or 'TRUE' (exactly as I would expect to do for ldap syntax), like:

```
              CHANGES: [('MODIFY_REPLACE', ['FALSE'])]
```

I think that it's really cool that reading the value into the Abstraction Layer I get back, for boolean type [1], a boolean value. But for consistency it should then let me also write on the attribute using boolean values. I would rather prefer a consistent behavior, let me use both for reading and for writing only string or only boolean.

[1] https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc4517#section-3.3.3

Hello,

I am using ldap3 1.0.4 to connect to two OpenLDAP servers with replication, configured as ServerPool with two entries, both created with `allowed_referral_hosts=[('*', True)]`.

When I call `extend.standard.modify_password` on connection to read-write server it executes successfully. On connection to read-only server, ldap3 receives response with referral from the server.

If this connection has auto_referrals=True, it raises an exception: 
`ldap3.core.exceptions.LDAPReferralError: referral operation not permitted`

If this connection has auto_referrals=False, it raises another exception: `ldap3.core.exceptions.LDAPExtensionError: extended operation error: referral -`

How do I make modify_password work with this configuration?

I have a server (OpenDJ) which is returning an object class definition with trailing space after the closing parenthesis, causing the recently-fixed schema validation to fail.

Do you know whether this whitespace is allowed by the RFC or not?

issue_title: [PATCH] RFC1213-MIB::atTable indices are not encoded/resolved correctly
issue_body:
Version: Branch master at commit 0f32c00d89eae2f86b2282ffc50805e059be5492
 
pysnmp/smi/mibs/RFC1213-MIB.py defines the `atNetAddress` index of `atTable` erroneously as `IpAddress`, while the correct type is `NetworkAddress`.  Since the two types have different OID encoding (`NetworkAddress` is equivalent to the modern `InetAddressType`-`InetAddress` combo and has an extra octet at the beginning which specifies the address type), PySNMP fails to encode or decode `atNetAddress` index.

To reproduce:

```
import pysnmp.debug
from pysnmp.smi.builder import MibBuilder
from pysnmp.smi.view import MibViewController
from pysnmp.smi.rfc1902 import ObjectIdentity
from pysnmp.proto.rfc1155 import IpAddress
from pysnmp.proto.rfc1902 import ObjectName
import user

builder = MibBuilder()
builder.loadModules('RFC1213-MIB')
view = MibViewController(builder)

pysnmp.debug.setLogger(pysnmp.debug.Debug('all'))

ident = ObjectIdentity('RFC1213-MIB', 'atIfIndex', 7, IpAddress('143.248.1.177'))
ident.resolveWithMib(view)
print(ident.getOid())

ident2 = ObjectIdentity(ObjectName('1.3.6.1.2.1.3.1.1.1.7.1.143.248.1.177'))
ident2.resolveWithMib(view)
print(ident2.getMibSymbol())
```

Results in:

```

2017-10-06 10:37:45,703 pysnmp: debug category 'all' enabled

2017-10-06 10:37:45,795 pysnmp: resolved ('RFC1213-MIB', 'atIfIndex', 7, IpAddress(hexValue='8ff801b1')) into prefix ObjectName('1.3.6.1.2.1.3.1.1.1') and suffix ()
2017-10-06 10:37:45,795 pysnmp: getNodeNameByOid: resolved :(1, 3, 6, 1, 2, 1, 3, 1, 1) -> ('iso', 'org', 'dod', 'internet', 'mgmt', 'mib-2', 'at', 'atTable', 'atEntry').()

2017-10-06 10:37:45,796 pysnmp: getNodeNameByOid: resolved :(1, 3, 6, 1, 2, 1, 3, 1, 1) -> ('iso', 'org', 'dod', 'internet', 'mgmt', 'mib-2', 'at', 'atTable', 'atEntry').()
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/opt/local/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.6/lib/python3.6/runpy.py", line 193, in _run_module_as_main
    "__main__", mod_spec)
  File "/opt/local/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.6/lib/python3.6/runpy.py", line 85, in _run_code

  File "/Users/eugene.kim/venvs/playground36/lib/python3.6/site-packages/pysnmp-4.4.1-py3.6.egg/pysnmp/smi/rfc1902.py", line 450, in resolveWithMib
  File "<string>", line 1223, in getIndicesFromInstId
pysnmp.smi.error.SmiError: Excessive instance identifier sub-OIDs left at MibTableRow((1, 3, 6, 1, 2, 1, 3, 1, 1), None): 177
```

Note:

- The OID-encoded `atNetAddress` `143.248.1.177` lacks the `NetworkAddress` type selector value `1` at the beginning;
- The buggy code could not resolve a correctly encoded index (`ident2`) because it did not expect the `NetworkAddress` type selector value before the IP address literal.  What happened instead is: 1) the buggy code erroneously decoded the address as `1.143.248.1` (the type selector value plus the first 3 sub-identifiers of the IP address), then 2) raised an exception upon seeing 177, which the last octet of the IP address.

`JSGStringMeta` does a pattern match against a the string representation of the target instance.  If the target instance is None, the match is applied to the string "None".  None should either map to the empty string or not map at all

```python
from pyjsg.jsglib.jsg import JSGString, JSGPattern
class S(JSGString):
    pattern = JSGPattern(r'[a-zA-Z]+')
    
isinstance('abc', S)
   True
isinstance('abc1', S)
   False
isinstance('', S)
  False
isinstance(None, S)
   True   # <--- Wrong
```

Hi Juan José,

I think there is a problem in the computation of gamma in your IAPWS97 library. I notice a factor 1000 between your IAPWS97 and IAPWS95 gamma values.
`fase.gamma = -self.v/self.P/1000*self.derivative("P", "v", "s", fase)`
should be :
`fase.gamma = -self.v/self.P*self.derivative("P", "v", "s", fase)`
You can also compute gamma as well if you do not want to use derivatives:
`fase.gamma = 1.e-6*self.w**2/self.P/self.v`           (if P is in MPa)
I use this last expression in my C code and the results are close to RefProp and IAPWS95 results.
Regards.

issue_title: SVG rendering in Jupyterlab doens't work for Firefox
issue_body:
I noticed that the SVG graphs wouldn't render properly in Jupyterlab under Firefox.

Some investigation and testing revealed that the `svg+xml` returned by the built-in `_repr_svg_` methods does not URL encode the `#` characters. This is apparently something that is required by the specifications, although Firefox seems to be the only one refusing to render the data. (See https://stackoverflow.com/questions/30733607/svg-data-image-not-working-on-firefox?answertab=active#tab-top for reference)

Adding a simple `.replace('#', '%23')` to the output of `_repr_svg_` seems to resolve the problem, although there might be a more elegant solution that I'm not aware of.

issue_title: Support multiple records of the same type on an identifier
issue_body:
In [certbot](https://github.com/certbot/certbot), we use lexicon to create TXT records for a domain to prove to a SSL/TLS certificate authority that the user has control over that domain so the CA will give you a certificate which you can use to set up HTTPS. Due to the way [the protocol](https://tools.ietf.org/html/draft-ietf-acme-acme-09) works, we sometimes have to set multiple TXT records for a single domain.

I'm not sure if each of the providers we use have problems with creating multiple TXT records on a domain (although I know this works with your code for dnsimple), but they all have problems deleting it and immediately error out if there is more than 1 record for the domain:

* [cloudxns](https://github.com/AnalogJ/lexicon/blob/a8c478ad44c06012c3aaaac077f70e67c12a136e/lexicon/providers/cloudxns.py#L125)
* [dnsimple](https://github.com/AnalogJ/lexicon/blob/903af58378ab9942d817c57e0330b5f7ac26b4e9/lexicon/providers/dnsimple.py#L128)
* [dnsmadeeasy](https://github.com/AnalogJ/lexicon/blob/9ad8a9cbf9e4b5c24fe525b274966c2432baf132/lexicon/providers/dnsmadeeasy.py#L121)
* [luadns](https://github.com/AnalogJ/lexicon/blob/a8c478ad44c06012c3aaaac077f70e67c12a136e/lexicon/providers/luadns.py#L98)
* [nsone](https://github.com/AnalogJ/lexicon/blob/8b649338b6c03c7bfb2d465249cd759de62296cb/lexicon/providers/nsone.py#L175)

Can this be fixed?

issue_title: get an Exception when you try to new a tab using 'logger' keyword
issue_body:
when you trying to new a tab like this:
`
Tab=crython.tab.CronTab(name='test',logger=logging.getLogger('xxx'))
`
it throws a Exception.
it is because you try to call the super function ,and thread.__init__() does't accept the keyword logger.
so you should pop the 'logger' keyword out ,the call super function

issue_title: A way to allow running the scheduled job immediately then every(x)?
issue_body:
Got this question via email, posting it here for reference:

> Is there a way to allow running the scheduled job immediately then every(x)?
>
> Something like this:
>
> schedule.every(10).minutes.do(start=datetime.now(), job)

I came across this project on HN and am putting together my mental team of projects for a software literate company - and wanted to understand a bit more.

Cron suffers from being fixed to one machine - whereas a company or organisation almost be default has more than one server and wants to load balance in some form

The first part (distributed job definitions) seems simple - text files can be as distributed as you like and brought together

The next stage is after the jobs have been scheduled, to have them sent out and run on more than one server - to have a grid, treat it as a grid and scheduler for it 

How amenable is the schedule package to that step (or is it already there and I can stop worrying!)

issue_title: ValueError: invalid literal for int() with base 10 in get
issue_body:
Operating System: MacOS X 10.11.5 (El Capitan)
Python versions: 2.7, 3.4, 3.5
Pydash version: 3.4.5
Reproduction steps:

1. In a Python virtualenv with pydash as a pip dependency, open a Python REPL and enter:
```
import pydash
a = { 'a': { 'b': { 'c': { 'd': [] } } } }
pydash.get(a, 'a.b.e.d', [])
``` 
2. You will get a TypeError stating that list indices must be integers, not str, followed by:
```
ValueError: invalid literal for int() with base 10: 'd'
```

It should have returned the default value `[]`

issue_title: git repo <target> create for group/organisations namespaces
issue_body: When creating a git repo for a group at Gitlab, I would want to call git-repo with the arguments `lab create <groupname>/<reponame>`, but this results in the repo being created for my current user, and not for the group.

This is what I experienced:
```
$ git repo hub add chmike/test origin
Successfully added `chmike/test` as remote named `github`
$ git remote -v
all	git@github.com:chmike/test (fetch)
all	git@github.com:chmike/test (push)
origin	git@github.com:chmike/test (fetch)
origin	git@github.com:chmike/test (push)
```
1. The feedback message say that a remote named `github` has been added where in fact it is named `origin`. 
2. Where is this `all` remote coming from ? I didn't ask for it. Why is it for ?

Here is more verbose output
```
$ git hub -vv add chmike/test test
> git remote get-url --all all
> Popen(['git', 'remote', 'get-url', '--all', 'all'], cwd=/tmp/test, universal_newlines=False, shell=None)
> git remote add test git@github.com:chmike/test
> Popen(['git', 'remote', 'add', 'test', 'git@github.com:chmike/test'], cwd=/tmp/test, universal_newlines=False, shell=None)
Successfully added `chmike/test` as remote named `github`
```

Once an `all` remote has been defined, git-repo does a `git remote get-url --all all`.

issue_title: How to handle cases with numberOfRecords == 0?
issue_body:
Dear Aymeric,

Unfortunately, I found a corner case in some of my files, that causes an numpy core records error:

```
lib/python2.7/site-packages/numpy/core/records.pyc in fromfile(fd, dtype, shape, offset, formats, names, titles, aligned, byteorder)
    781     nbytesread = fd.readinto(_array.data)
    782     if nbytesread != nbytes:
--> 783         raise IOError("Didn't read as many bytes as expected")
    784     if name:
    785         fd.close()
```
It is triggered, when numberOfRecords == 0.
In the `readSortedRecord` function, I'm in the case channelList is None and self.byte_aligned. Then the call
`return fromfile(fid, dtype=self.numpyDataRecordFormat, shape=self.numberOfRecords, names=self.dataRecordName)` throws the numpy error.

Do you have a suggestion how to handle this corner case? It is a measurement, which is written to file, but no data is actually recorded.

Thanks,
Katharina

issue_title: query rate limit exceeded failures can cause RecursionError failures
issue_body:
I recently upgraded to python 3.5 and reinstalled the ipwhois package. That upgraded my ipwhois package from 0.10.1 to 0.13.0. I'm now seeing these failures:

```
RecursionError: maximum recursion depth exceeded in comparison
```

Specifically,

```
  File "/Users/krader/conda/envs/py35/lib/python3.5/site-packages/ipwhois/ipwhois.py", line 146, in lookup_whois
    ignore_referral_errors, asn_data, field_list
  File "/Users/krader/conda/envs/py35/lib/python3.5/site-packages/ipwhois/whois.py", line 552, in lookup
    extra_blacklist=extra_blacklist)
  File "/Users/krader/conda/envs/py35/lib/python3.5/site-packages/ipwhois/net.py", line 573, in get_whois
    extra_blacklist)
  File "/Users/krader/conda/envs/py35/lib/python3.5/site-packages/ipwhois/net.py", line 573, in get_whois
    extra_blacklist)
... etcetera ...
```

The problem is that 'Query rate limit exceeded' errors call `self.get_whois()` recursively without decrementing `retry_count` as happens for socket level exceptions.
Suggest adding integration to Slack such that once an experiment is running, output is streamed either in detail or summary to a slack channel that the experimenter can monitor.
Does this have support for ipv6?

issue_title: Unable to issue credentials for different role if current credentials have no expired
issue_body: If the current credentials are that of an assumed role and have not expired, you are unable to assume the role of a different account. This is because the the application begins with validation that only checks the expiration date. You'll need to add logic to check and see if a current role is assumed and if the provided role is different, in which case you'll need to fetch new credentials.

issue_title: Endless loop (100% CPU) with shlex.split completion
issue_body:
The following script will cause 100% CPU:

```python
import jedi

script = """import shlex
qsplit = shlex.split("foo, ferwerwerw werw werw e")

issue_title: Windows: provide APIs to deal with Windows services
issue_body:
Rationale
=======

People who want to deal with Windows services in Python are forced to rely either on pywin32 or WMI extensions but they're too low level (pywin32) and it's not immediately clear what to use and how to use it. Some examples:

- list() / wmi: http://www.thecodingforums.com/threads/listing-existing-windows-services-with-python.715123/
- create() / pywin32:  http://code.activestate.com/recipes/576451-how-to-create-a-windows-service-in-python/
- remove() / pywin32: https://bytes.com/topic/python/answers/32671-win32serviceutil-remove-service

It would be nice if psutil could provide a set of APIs to easily deal with Windows services.

API
===

I'm still not sure how to organize the API (classes? functions? namedtuple? etc.) yet, but roughly this is what we're looking at:

- list()
- create()
- remove()
- modify()
- start() / stop() / restart()

References
=========

- remove() and create(): http://web.mit.edu/source-8.1/third/xntp/scripts/wininstall/instsrv/instsrv.c

issue_title: Problems with network statistics on Solaris 10
issue_body:
We’ve been evaluating psutil for suitability in a cross-platform machine statistic gathering application that we maintain for internal use. We’ve had several issues on Solaris and the attached patch file contains changes that made to get it working on Solaris 10.
 
* We didn’t have any kstat network results where ks_module == “link”, so we hacked it to look for ks_name == “mac”. There is probably a better solution. The “kstat -c net”  output from several of our servers is linked below for reference. Our servers have multi-port NIC cards, so we needed to mangle ks_name + ks_instance as the key of the returned dictionary.
* The code was compiled as 32-bit code to match our Python installation. The #if code assumes that the kstat statistics will match the number of bits used to compile. That is not a valid assumption. A hack was made to check at runtime.

This patch is not ready to merge, but I would be happy to get feedback to make it acceptable.
 
Patch - https://gist.github.com/lymanepp/c5e403df4cbd73561d9a
Kstat output (server 1) - https://gist.github.com/lymanepp/2d105d01da798760f2c5
Kstat output (server 2) - https://gist.github.com/lymanepp/2b6c103ff97fdf5995f2

issue_title: XEP-0092: Correctly handle requirement of name and version in result
issue_body:
@sebastianriese correctly pointed out that XEP-0092 REQUIRES that ``<name/>`` and ``<version/>`` are included in a result.

We have three options:

* Force ``<name/>`` and ``<version/>`` to be the defaults (``"aioxmpp"``/``aioxmpp.__version__``) instead of None when deleted.
* Do not answer queries if either is set to None (reply with ``<service-unavailable/>``)
* Set them to ``"unspecified"``, ``"unspecified"`` instead.

Opinions?

Although this is not in the docs anywhere, in one of your tests, you have `os.fork()` and you're running `bjoern.run()` multiple times.

What does this actually do?

Is the `bjoern` event loop single threaded (but it is not event driven like Node.js)? So does that mean normally it can only handle 1 request at a time, because the WSGI app will not yield back to the python scheduler? Therefore to get the best performance we need to run multiple `bjoern.run()` so that requests can be handled in parallel in multiple Python processes.

I'm hoping to get more documentation on the exact behaviour of bjoern in the presence of Python multithreading and its GIL lock, and multiprocessing as in the case of using `os.fork()`.

issue_title: Add a default Protocol class for JoinGroup Structs
issue_body:
In the JoinGroup structs, the consumer can pass a protocol that it supports. However, if you read the underlying KIP, it says: 
> The primary use case for the membership API is consumer groups, but the requests are intentionally generic to support other cases (e.g. Kafka Connect groups). The cost of this generality is that specific group semantics are pushed into the client. For example, the JoinGroup/SyncGroup requests defined below have no explicit fields supporting partition assignment for consumer groups. Instead, they contain generic byte arrays in which assignments can be embedded by the consumer client implementation.
>
> But although this allows each client implementation to define its own embedded schema, compatibility with Kafka tooling requires clients to use the standard embedded schema used by the client shipped with Kafka. The consumer-groups.sh utility, for example, assumes this format to display partition assignments. We therefore recommend that clients should follow the same schema so that these tools will work for all client implementations.

Since the assumption is that all clients implement the same protocol as Kafka's Java client, it'd be good to add this as a class var in the JoinGroup structs... this would make it easier to use the JoinGroup structs with the low-level `KafkaClient` by just specifying the default protocol.

A simple consumer program like

    consumer = KafkaConsumer('t1',bootstrap_servers='bootstrap-servers',
                                                     enable_auto_commit=True,
                                                     auto_commit_interval_ms=30 * 1000,
                                                     group_id='mygroup')

    for msg in consumer:
           print(msg.offset)
           process(msg.value)

is not saving the offset in kafka. If for some reason the program quits and is restarted, it is starting over from offset 0 rather then where it was when it was stopped.

issue_title: Document storage backend requirements for compatibility
issue_body: It would be helpful to add the requirements of the storage backend that whitenoise uses to the documentation for subclassing.  For example, does it require that the storage backend supply a local file path, or could it use a remote storage backend?

Hello - First of all, thank you for developing and maintaining whitenoise. Making the leap from an S3-based storage to whitenoise proved to solve *many* headaches, and has only introduced some very minor ones in the process. ;-) We love it!

We're currently experiencing an issue with 3.3.0 not being able to determine that a static file is immutable when it actually is. I've done a bit of digging into this and it looks like it's because our public facing URL doesn't have the static prefix in it.

Our setup:

- CDN:
  - Public URL: `https://foo/path/to/asset.abc123.js`
  - Origin URL: `https://foo/static/path/to/asset.abc123.js`

- Django:
  - STATIC_URL: `https://foo/`
  - WHITENOISE_STATIC_PREFIX: `/static/`

[The whitenoise code](https://github.com/evansd/whitenoise/blob/bc8fe1edb7a350607b2d40104d3ce9b8e2e22b16/whitenoise/django.py#L98-L102) that evaluates to the immutability test to False:

```
        # If the static URL function maps the name without hash
        # back to the original URL, then we know we've got a
        # versioned filename
        if static_url and static_url.endswith(url):
            return True
```

I note that the code remains the same on the 4.0 release candidates, so this is probably a good time to reconsider this. It seems like the code should either only be looking at the `basename` for the file when performing the check, or perhaps it should integrate the `WHITENOISE_STATIC_PREFIX` value into the url prior to checking. Thoughts?

This is currently making our production website not cache assets for more than 60 seconds, so we'll probably put *some* sort-of fix in today, but it'd be great to get a permanent fix in too. :-)

Thanks!

I wrote a plugin for this. It hides repeated-text (and it's table cell borders) in vertical columns. Its another way of 'grouping' content together (except that you use that word for something else. Its triggered by a class in the <th>. It works by adding a class to the td

So, a table like

```
a a a
a a b
a a c
a b a
a b b
b a a
```

Gets displayed as

```
a a a
    b
    c
a b a
    b
b a a
```

Best to get the idea with an example :  

https://www.walkingclub.org.uk/walk/
https://www.walkingclub.org.uk/hills/munros/index.shtml

The CSS I use is

```
    .sp_col_show {
        background-color: white !important;  /* needed because of stripped rows */
        }

    .sp_col_hide {
        visibility: hidden;
        border-top: white !important;
        background-color: white !important; /* needed because of stripped rows */

```

Sometimes it may be useful to organise packages into projects and analyse a whole project at a time.

Index files kind of solve this issue, by defining an index file for each project, but the analysis results overlap. Haros always uses the same analysis database file. Adding an option to load a different file could also solve this, at the expense of making the command-line a little more cumbersome.

In any case, the visualiser is not adapted for this concept, so multiple visualisers would have to be exported, one for each project. This requires launching multiple viz servers (when hosting), or creating a top-level `index.html` with links to open the individual projects, which is not ideal.

My proposal is to add a new `Project` data structure.

```python
class Project(object):
    def __init__(self, name, packages = None):
        self.name = name
        self.packages = packages if not packages is None else []
```

Index files could define a `name` for a project, or default to the file name minus extension.
The `AnalysisManager` could store the history for each project separately, or simply create one analysis database for each project. The exported *viz* files and directory structure would have to change. Instead of `data/packages.json`, etc., it would have to go one level deeper, `data/project/packages.json`. Viz itself would have to be changed as well.

issue_title: Support keyboard media keys (for brightness and volume)
issue_body:
I use `backlight` and `volume_status`, but even though both of them have functionality to change the value, it is only accessible via mouse click/scroll, not via keyboard.

My keyboard has dedicated media buttons for adjusting brightness and volume, I want to use them, it is faster than reaching to the mouse.

That's why I currently don't use functionality from the py3status module, but instead I reimplemented the functionality myself and have the following config in my `.i3/config`:

```
bindsym XF86AudioMute         exec --no-startup-id ~/bin/set-volume.sh mute
bindsym XF86AudioRaiseVolume  exec --no-startup-id ~/bin/set-volume.sh +5
bindsym XF86AudioLowerVolume  exec --no-startup-id ~/bin/set-volume.sh -5
bindsym XF86MonBrightnessUp   exec --no-startup-id ~/bin/set-brightness.sh inc
bindsym XF86MonBrightnessDown exec --no-startup-id ~/bin/set-brightness.sh dec
```

Two problems with this:

- This prevents code sharing and collaboration. Today every person who wants to use keyboard has to stop using py3status for adjusting the values and reimplement the same logic in their own scripts.
- The scripts that I made cannot refresh only one module, they have to issue `killall -USR1 py3status`, but this is very inefficient and when I press e.g. "volume down" button several times repeatedly, my CPU usage reaches 100%.

-------

We can of course extend this to a more generic "support any keyboard shortcuts", but I decided to start with this smaller and more specific example.

MVP idea:

- [ ] just two algorithms
- [ ] average them together
- [ ] no dataset modification- same dataset for both

MVP implementation

- [ ] we'll still need our own Ensembler class (might even be able to pull this from the previous codebase)
- [ ] this class should also have an option to return the predictions from all the models
- [ ] this class should basically have two main components
    - [ ] getting the predictions from each model from the same already-feature-engineered data
    - [ ] somehow assembling all these predictions together into a single prediction
the first part should be pretty fixed- implement it once, and it's good to go. the second part will likely vary quite a bit more over time


post-MVP implementation

- [ ] allow the user to modify the dataset for a given model (feed in only a subset of the data for a given model)
this should be relatively easy.
    - [ ] fit the transformation pipeline on the entire training dataset
    - [ ] then, allow the user to perform their filtering on the raw input
    - [ ] once they've filtered, we pass whatever they return through the already trained transformation pipeline, just transforming it this time (not fitting it, obviously)
- [ ] ensemble the predictions together algorithmically
    - [ ] allow the user to pass in additional configs for what model to choose, whether to optimize this model, and force them to pass in separate training data, and ensembler_training_data. 
no additional feature engineering function from the user here, though we will likely do a bunch of our own feature engineering (min, max, median, each prediction multiplied by it's accuracy at training time or something, etc.)

the key point to making all this run at production speeds:

print_all_features
print_bad_features

print_bad_features will print a list of the features that were not much help for that algorithm, or that were not selected by feature_selection

print_all_features will just print out all the features, where right now we're just printing out the top 50.

I wanted to load a sound file from an URL and had a look at the example in the README:

```Python
from io import BytesIO
import soundfile as sf
import requests

f = BytesIO()
response = requests.get('http://www.example.com/my.flac', stream=True)
for data in response.iter_content(4096):
    if data:
        f.write(data)
f.seek(0)
data, samplerate = sf.read(f)
```

I found out that this has a lot of shortcomings:

1. the URL doesn't exist
2. there is no meaningful exception message if the URL doesn't exist
3. it needs an external library (`requests`)
4. the for-loop looks very complicated for such a simple task
5. the if-clause within looks strange
6. the "stream" stuff gives false hope that PySoundFile might support streaming from file objects, which it doesn't

Point 1: I don't know a good example URL. The closest I could find are the WAV files here http://www.cs.bath.ac.uk/~rwd/cardattrit.html
Does anyone know a publicly available sound file with a nice URL?

Point 2 and 3 would be solved if we switch to the Python standard library.
The disadvantage would be that we have to take special care because the imports for Python 2 and 3 are different.
For me it would also be OK if we make the example Python-3-only.

Point 4, 5 and 6 can be avoided if we simply get the whole content (as bytes) from the HTTP library and shove it into a `BytesIO`.
Starting with CPython 3.5, there will be even one less copy because of an optimization in `BytesIO` (http://bugs.python.org/issue22003).

Here is my suggestion:

```Python
import io
import soundfile as sf
try:
    # Python 3.x
    from urllib.request import urlopen
except ImportError:
    # Python 2.x
    from urllib2 import urlopen

url = "http://www.cs.bath.ac.uk/~rwd/stereol.wav"
data, samplerate = sf.read(io.BytesIO(urlopen(url).read()))
```

I don't see a reason why we should need an external library for such a simple task, but just for completeness' sake, while still using `requests`, points 4, 5 and 6 could be avoided like this:

```Python
import requests
data, samplerate = sf.read(io.BytesIO(requests.get(url).content))
```

issue_title: Please use explicit optional parameter `loop=None` everywhere in API

issue_body:
The `current_user.has_roles()` functionality is as follows:
- `has_roles('A', 'B')` returns True if the user has both role 'A' **and** role 'B' 
- `has_roles(['A', 'B'])` returns True if the user has either role 'A' **or** role 'B'

Most developers consider this syntax counter-intuitive.

Instead of changing functionality and breaking backward compatibility, we propose adding a `has_role()` function (singular 'has_role' vs plural 'has_roles'):
- `has_role('A', 'B')` returns True if the user has either role 'A' **or** role 'B'

In addition to @roles_required() as an AND operation, we could also offer a @roles_accepted() as an OR operation

Scheduled for v1.0.

hi,

thanks for SoundScrape!

i use it with the `-f` option. sometimes a download hangs and i have to interrupt the script (usually ctrl-c). this leaves an incomplete, corrupted download file in the local library, causing two issues:

- my player will attempt to play the corrupted download.
- more importantly, when i retry the `soundscrape -f` command, **the corrupted download is skipped entirely** (instead of being retried or continued) leaving the corrupted file in the library forever.

these issues are easy to fix. a workable solution is:

- use a temporary filename while downloading files. make sure the extension does not match any recognizable music extension to lessen the chance that a player will pick it up. if applicable, lock the file against reading by other processes for the same reason. downloading to /tmp or equivalent can also be considered.
-  if the temporary filename generation policy can result in clashes with previous download attempts, then silently overwrite any existing temporary files.
- for even cleaner behavior, register a termination callback or equivalent to remove the temporary file currently being downloaded if there is one. (ideally ctrl-c should remove the temp download.)

thanks!

issue_title: Feature request: Add option to avoid reading chart cache data
issue_body:
By default, and unseen to the user, the chart.py module reads cell data from the worksheets that charts series refer to and stores it as cached data values in the chart.

This isn't strictly required by Excel but it increased compatibility and ensures that charts are rendered correctly by third part apps and in particular by PowerPoint.

However, for certain complex ranges the data reference can't (easily) be parsed. The currently workaround is to specify populated or empty data lists:

```python
chart.add_series({'categories':      '=chart.xlsx!Categories1', 
                  'values':          '=chart.xlsx!Series2',
                  'categories_data': [], 
                  'values_data':     []})
````
A cleaner solution may be to add an option to explicitly not try to cache data:
```python
chart.add_series({'categories': '=chart.xlsx!Categories1', 
                  'values':     '=chart.xlsx!Series2',
                  'cached_data': False})
````

issue_title: Investigate a new hash160 indexing scheme
issue_body:
Index by hash160(script_pubkey).  i.e.:

P2PKH: hash160('76a9' + hash160(pubkey) + '88ac')
P2SH: hash160('a9' + hash160(redeem_script) + '87')
P2PubKey: hash160(pubkey + 'ac')

and all strange scripts too.  Would save a byte and be generic.

```
grep -P -e "elec" /var/log/ulog/syslogemu.log | grep -P -o -e"DPT=[0-9]+" | sort -u
DPT=443
DPT=50003
DPT=50011
DPT=50012
DPT=57082
DPT=995
```

(these are logs for explicit reject rules applied for outgoing new packets from `electrumx` linux user that runs `electrumx_server`).

Please note `443` and `995` ports. In enterprise environment, this could be seen as "botnet-like" behavior. 

It would be useful to have option set allowed peer port range, so peers with non-standart (not 50001-50002) ports would be ignored and connections not established with them. By default, all ports should be allowed, of course.

This is not "nice" for peers that want to "hide" electrum server usage (like these with 443 or 995 ports), but this option would be used by some in specific environments.

issue_title: Unclear semantics on how to correlate response with request
issue_body:
The rendezvous server protocol is full duplex. The client can receive messages from the server at any time, and the server can receive messages from the client at any time.

However, some messages that originate from the client have RPC semantics. e.g. `list` or `allocate` (or even `ping`). These messages are requests that need a response.

AFAICT, the Python client assumes that the first response of the right type is the intended response for the message. e.g. the first `allocated` message after an `allocate` request must be the response.

If this is the intended semantics of the protocol, then server-protocol.md should be updated accordingly.

These are OK semantics, but they mean that only one concurrent thread of execution can use a wormhole connection at a time (is this my `allocate` response, or someone else's?)

Perhaps a better approach would be to elevate the importance of the `id` field, making it the way to correlate request & response. This would require fixing #259, and ideally renaming the `ping` and `pong` fields to `id`.

issue_title: A way to copy an instance while replacing some fields
issue_body:
When programming with an immutable/frozen data structure (which I personally prefer to do whenever it's reasonable), perhaps the most common operation is to replace some subset of the fields with new values, returning a new instance. It would be nice if dataclasses had similar functionality, for frozen classes at least.

Some prior art:

---

Python 3 dict:

```python
a = {'key1': 'value1', 'key2': 'value2'}
b = {**a, 'key1': 'VALUE1'}
```

----

namedtuple [_replace](https://docs.python.org/3.6/library/collections.html#collections.somenamedtuple._replace):

```python
NT = namedtuple('NT', ('field1', 'field2'))
a = NT(field1='value1', field2='value2')
b = a._replace(field1='VALUE1')
```

---

attrs [evolve](http://www.attrs.org/en/stable/api.html#attr.evolve):

```python
@attr.s
class C:
     field1 = attr.ib()
     field2 = attr.ib()
a = C(field1='value1', field2='value2')
b = attr.evolve(field1='VALUE1')
```

---

Kotlin data class [copy](https://kotlinlang.org/docs/reference/data-classes.html#copying):

```kotlin
data class C(val field1: String, val field2: String)
val a = C(field1 = "value1", field2 = "value2")
val b = a.copy(field1 = "VALUE1")
```

---

Clojure record [assoc](https://clojuredocs.org/clojure.core/assoc):

```clojure
(defrecord R [field1 field2])
(def a (R. "value1" "value2"))
(def b (assoc a :field1 "VALUE1"))
```

---

There are of course other example, but I think this shows the variety of names.

issue_title: modified_time in s3boto3 raises ValueError: astimezone() cannot be applied to a naive datetime
issue_body:
In the [recent additions](https://github.com/jschneier/django-storages/commit/302aa743c621f7d801142ac54c825aabed8c1125) to the `s3boto3` TZ handling, running with `USE_TZ = False` under Django<1.10 (which calls `modified_time`), the following traceback occurs:

```
...
File "./django/contrib/staticfiles/management/commands/collectstatic.py", line 305, in copy_file
    if not self.delete_file(path, prefixed_path, source_storage):
  File "./django/contrib/staticfiles/management/commands/collectstatic.py", line 222, in delete_file
    self.storage.modified_time(prefixed_path)
  File "./storages/backends/s3boto3.py", line 537, in modified_time
    return localtime(self.get_modified_time(name)).replace(tzinfo=None)
  File "./site-packages/django/utils/timezone.py", line 309, in localtime
    value = value.astimezone(timezone)
ValueError: astimezone() cannot be applied to a naive datetime
```

This is a result of `get_modified_time` returning a naive datetime (as a result of `USE_TZ = False`), which `modified_time` then attempts to again convert to a localized time via `localtime`.

issue_title: Model FilterSets should include foreign key integerfields.
issue_body:
Taking these models:

```
class Place(models.Model):
    name = models.CharField(max_length=50)
    address = models.CharField(max_length=80)

class Restaurant(models.Model):
    place = models.OneToOneField(Place, primary_key=True)
    serves_hot_dogs = models.BooleanField(default=False)
    serves_pizza = models.BooleanField(default=False)
```

`RestaurantFilterSet` should then include both children `place` filterset and `place_id`.

Doing that will allow to optimize filtering by not causing unnecessary joins for urls like:

```
place_id=5
vs
place__id=5 == place=5
```
It seems if a string begins with a space titlecase will treat that space as the first word in the title.  For example, ' to turn a tide' will end up ' to Turn a Tide' instead of ' To Turn a Tide'.

**Latex**
No. Barely readable syntax.

**Report Lab**
No. Way to low level. I don't care about pixel alignment.

[**xhtml2pdf**](https://github.com/chrisglass/xhtml2pdf)
Promising. Simple. [Works with flask](http://flask.pocoo.org/snippets/68/).

[**WeasyPrint**](https://github.com/Kozea/WeasyPrint)
Very promising. First choice candidate. Has better CSS support than other options. [Flask support](https://pythonhosted.org/Flask-WeasyPrint/).

Other points:
- It's perfectly possible to render Jinja2 to HTML without a server.

issue_title: Interpretation of P(alive) for one-time customers
issue_body:
I've noticed a strange behaviour in the behaviour of the `conditional_probability_alive` method of the `ModifiedBetaGeoFitter` class. I'm not sure if it's a shortcoming in the model or implementation, a data issue or a mistake I'm making, but I'm hoping someone has seen this type of result before and can help me along.

The problem lies in the distributions I'm getting for the values of P(alive) across customers. The following plot illustrates the issue:
![screenshot from 2018-03-16 23-58-16](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/734695/37549237-f71d4194-2975-11e8-8f4b-51e8401118d8.png)

The fairly smooth behaviour for high values of P(alive) is intuitively understandable, but the bulge around P(alive)=0.3 and the sharp cutoff is unexpected. Inspection reveals that the bulge is composed of customers who have made a single purchase, since it disappears when plotting only repeat customers:
![screenshot from 2018-03-16 23-58-25](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/734695/37549243-fcbb16ee-2975-11e8-827c-e1f4e34447fa.png)

The cliff on the left side of the bulge is composed of customers who made their single purchase near the beginning of the observation period, and the customers get more recent as you move right within the bulge.

This distribution of P(alive) for one-time customers doesn't seem to me to be an accurate or useful representation of these customers' probability of being alive, but the issue crops up across datasets so I'm wondering if:

- There is anything incorrect or implausible about my interpretation
- This is a known issue with this type of model
- There are any known workarounds or fixes

Any other thoughts or suggestions would be very welcome.

issue_title: Using an empty API prefix (the default) gives a swagger schema with basePath of '/'
issue_body:
First, thanks for such a great framework! I've just used it to create an API, and have hit a problem when trying to access it with a client application.

Create an API without specifying any `prefix` argument to `flask_restplus.Api`, and then `api.__schema__`  (the swagger/OpenAPI spec) contains an entry: `"basePath": "/"`. 

This caused me some problems because a strict reading of the [OpenAPI standard for paths](https://github.com/OAI/OpenAPI-Specification/blob/master/versions/2.0.md#paths-object) says this:
> A relative path to an individual endpoint. The field name MUST begin with a slash. The path is appended to the basePath in order to construct the full URL. Path templating is allowed.

So applying this exactly as it says: any path, which must begin with a `/`, say `/example`, becomes `//example` when appended to the `basePath`, which Flask will not treat the same way as `/example`, and in my case `//example` redirected to the swagger UI running at `/` and that caused carnage.

Reading the [OpenAPI field description for the `basePath` entry](https://github.com/OAI/OpenAPI-Specification/blob/master/versions/2.0.md#fixed-fields):
> The base path on which the API is served, which is relative to the host. If it is not included, the API is served directly under the host. The value MUST start with a leading slash (/). The basePath does not support path templating.

The solution to this is to not include any `basePath` if it would otherwise be `/`.

For now, I've had to work around this by specifying a `prefix` argument to `flask_restplus.Api` of `prefix='/api'` and have all my APIs under there, because that gives a `basePath` of `/api`, and correctly formatted paths, e.g. `/api/example`.

I skimmed the code here, and I think a quick fix would be a `if len(basepath) == 1: basepath = None` [here](https://github.com/noirbizarre/flask-restplus/blob/master/flask_restplus/swagger.py#L136-L138) and then the `basePath` entry would be removed by the `not_none` call [here](https://github.com/noirbizarre/flask-restplus/blob/master/flask_restplus/swagger.py#L183).

I'm happy to try and submit a patch for this, but from a quick look it's going to break a bunch of [tests](https://github.com/noirbizarre/flask-restplus/blob/master/tests/test_swagger.py#L34) so I thought I'd run it past you first.

Thanks for your time :)

issue_title: Allow adding separate where='sql expression' for PostgreSQL and SQLite
issue_body:
The where='sql expression' is inserted directly into the CREATE INDEX statement when a migration runs. It must be valid for the currently active database backend.

While the django-partial-index project supports both PostgreSQL and SQLite, they have slightly different expected syntax for some expressions.

In a particularly common case, they differ in valid boolean literals: SQLite only accepts 0 and 1 as numbers, and PostgreSQL only accepts TRUE, FALSE and a few string values: https://www.postgresql.org/docs/9.1/static/datatype-boolean.html

This means that where-expressions for the common case of filtering on a boolean flag need two different expressions in a project that uses PostgreSQL in production and SQLite in testing.

Proposed solution:
* where='' is used for both backends,
* where_postgresql='' and where_sqlite='' can be used instead to provide different expressions,
* Only one of them can be provided if the developer is not aiming to support both backends,
* It's up to the developer to ensure that their behaviour is identical,
* For consistency, where='' cannot be used if either or both where_<vendor>='' are provided,
* For consistency, where_<vendor>='' expressions must be different; if they are identical you must use where=''.

issue_title: Wrap DOT/PSA views in DRF views
issue_body:
Hi, me again :)

If I understand correctly, the aim of this package is to provide seamless integration of `django-oauth-toolkit` and `python-social-auth` with Django REST framework. This is why, I believe that this library should wrap around all the views that imports from DOT and PSA, instead of [importing](https://github.com/PhilipGarnero/django-rest-framework-social-oauth2/blob/master/rest_framework_social_oauth2/urls.py#L4) them directly in `urls.py`.

Why is this important? Because by having all views inherit at least from DRF's `APIView` we allow the renderers and all other DRF related stuff kick in. For example I have a renderer that wraps all responses in `data` and adds `pagination` dict if necessary, but it will never kick in because DOT returns raw Django responses. Additionally this would allow this library to catch the raw errors coming from DOT/PSA/Oauthlib and have them go through DRF's exception handler -> return uniform error responses.

This would broaden the scope of the library, but I also believe would make it much more valuable. As there is no library out there which provides complete and seamless integration of OAuth with Django REST framework.

I try to create an object via a POST request. This works very well, now to my problem: 

I want the response to have the field expanded by default (in the direct response to the post request). 
When trying this either via `http://localhost:8000/api/cities?expand=country` or setting `extensions_expand = {'country'}` on the view (my preferred way), i get a validation error `"country": ["This field is required." ]`.

So using `extensions_expand={'country'}` results in an error on POST (and also PUT i think). I can bypass this error by creating using `get_extensions_mixin_context` on the view

```
 def get_extensions_mixin_context(self):
        context = super(CityViewSet, self).get_extensions_mixin_context()
        if self.request.method in ['POST', 'PUT']:
            context['expand'] = set()
        return context
``` 
This works at least, but still doesn't produce the desired result of having `country` by expanded in the post response. 

I also have a repo ready for reproduction: https://github.com/BerniWittmann/drf_serializer_ext
Just try to post something to `localhost:8000/api/cities`. The desired response would be: 

```
{
        "url": "http://localhost:8000/api/cities/1/",
        "name": "Berlin",
        "id": 1,
        "country": {
            "url": "http://localhost:8000/api/countries/1/",
            "name": "Germany",
            "id": 1,
            "code": "DE"
        },
        "country_id": 1
}
```

If anything is unclear, let me know :)

issue_title: Patch to fix bugs in FreeBSD
issue_body:
The problem reported here [1], which affects FreeBSD, apparently in version 10, is corrected by applying the following patch to 

file /web2py/gluon/packages/dal/pydal/contrib/portalocker.py

--- portalocker.py.old	2015-05-30 13:33:10.000000000 -0300
+++ portalocker.py	2015-06-25 15:46:24.000000000 -0300
@@ -119,7 +119,7 @@
             lock(self.file, LOCK_EX)
             if not 'a' in mode:
                 self.file.seek(0)
-                self.file.truncate()
+                self.file.truncate(0)
         else:
             raise RuntimeError("invalid LockedFile(...,mode)")


[1] https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/web2py/E-E1E1pdkYY

issue_body:
Any time I call Keras model.train method, there is an illegal instruction error.  I can reproduce this behavior with any/all the Keras examples.

## Environment info
Operating System:

## Minimum reproducible example/steps to reproduce
From your clone of the Keras repo, examples directory, run any of the example from the command line.  (`python addition_rnn.py` for example).

Currently `epoch_end_callback` and `batch_end_callback` have two types of interfaces, this make things like logging evaluation metric not compatible. The things presented to the callbacks are also somewhat limited.

I like the way R handles the problem, where an environment variable is passed, which basically is a map containing all the local variables in the scope, this issue is opened to see if anyone likes to move toward this direction, and take stab on proposing specification of function in this issue, and contribute the code

issue_title: Using NDArrayIter causes Huge Memory Usage
issue_body:
I've got a network that I'm trying to train, but I'm running into weird memory issues. When using the following code...
```
t = softmax.simple_bind(ctx=mxnet.cpu(),
                            data=(10, 3, 227, 227))

print(t.debug_str())
```

It suggests I'll need about 250MB of memory for this. When training the network using NDArrayIter, it uses over 40GB of ram (64GB machine)!

Ideally I'm wanting to use my GPU, but I'm sure it's pretty obvious that my GPU does not have that sort of RAM.

My iterators are defined as follows, where exemplars_train is (7878, 3, 227, 227), and exemplars_test is (2623, 3, 227, 227):

```
train_iter = mxnet.io.NDArrayIter(exemplars_train, labels_train, 10)
test_iter = mxnet.io.NDArrayIter(exemplars_test, labels_test, 10)
```

And the fitting of the model for training is as follows:

```
model = mxnet.model.FeedForward(
	ctx = mxnet.cpu(0),
	symbol=softmax,
	num_epoch=epochs,
	learning_rate = 0.9)

eval_metrics = ['accuracy']

model.fit(
	X=train_iter,
	eval_data=test_iter,
	eval_metric = eval_metrics,
	epoch_end_callback = mxnet.callback.do_checkpoint("chks2/train", 10),
	batch_end_callback = mxnet.callback.Speedometer(100,10))
```

Is it normal to have this amount of memory usage? It seems extraordinarily high to me. As I mentioned above, I want to get this training on my GPU, but with things currently, this doesn't seem like it will be a possibility. Even if I set the batch size to 1, it is still too much for my GPU.

So my questions regarding this are as follows:

- Is this amount of memory usage normal?
- Am I using NDArrayIter wrong?
- Is there another type of iterator I can use with less memory footprint if this is normal?

Thanks in advance.

issue_title: Incorrect implied shape inside loss function
issue_body:
Ive seen this brought up in a couple other issues, but it hasnt been resolved as far as I know.  

The data I am feeding into my loss function is of the following shape (batch size 32):

```
output.shape (32L, 51L)
label.shape (32L, 51L)
```

When the `output` and `label` are fed into the loss function, tho, I get the following error:

`MXNetError: Shape inconsistent, Provided=(32,51), inferred shape=(32,1)`

Why is the loss function implying an incorrect shape?  In the line above said loss function, Gluon knows the correct shape of each input matrix, but the loss function auto-implies the shape incorrectly.

issue_title: [Feature Request] Gluon model zoo allow fine-tuning
issue_body:
Currently it is a little bit of a faff to fine-tune a model from the model-zoo.
The straight dope takes the option of downloading both the pre-trained and non-trained model and assigning the trained features to the non-trained model.
http://gluon.mxnet.io/chapter08_computer-vision/fine-tuning.html
As done in the naming tutorial, you can also re assign the `.output` layer of the model from the model zoo but that again requires knowledge of the code.

I propose that when calling:
```python
vision.resnet18_v1(pretrained=True, classes=10)
```
instead of getting an error with:
```
AssertionError: Failed loading Parameter 'resnetv10_dense0_weight' from saved params: shape incompatible expected (10, 512) vs saved (1000, 512)
```
We get the pre-trained model with the last layer replaced with a 10-unit dense layer, which makes more sense from a user perspective.

issue_title: UX for ONNX Documentation is broken

issue_body:
I am fairly new to mxnet so I could be missing something here.
From what I can glean, the facilities for ndarray and symbolic tensor manipulations lack built-in support for aggregations along tensor dimensions. E.g. mean, standard deviation, sum, product...
In theano for example, I can do x.sum(axis=1) in a symbolic graph and all the forward/backward operations are handled properly.
What would be the equivalent thing to do in mxnet at this time?
Suppose for example I want to include a mean and variance normalization step on top of the activities of a given network layer as part of the graph.

issue_title: Incorrect formatting on IPv6 address when run with -6
issue_body:
When I run: `manage.py runserver_plus 8080 -6 --cert C:\cert\`

The output is:
```
 * Restarting with stat
Performing system checks...

System check identified no issues (0 silenced).

Django version 2.0, using settings 'test.settings'
Development server is running at https://::1:8080/
Using the Werkzeug debugger (http://werkzeug.pocoo.org/)
Quit the server with CTRL-BREAK.
 * Debugger is active!
 * Debugger PIN: 129-007-922
 * Running on https://[::1]:8080/ (Press CTRL+C to quit)
```

The line `Development server is running at https://::1:8080/`

Should be `Development server is running at https://[::1]:8080/`

Hi there,

This [line](https://github.com/facebook/facebook-python-ads-sdk/blob/master/facebookads/typechecker.py#L210-L216) in the `TypeChecker` class makes it impossible to define custom classes. issue_title: jQuery.parseJSON requires a valid JSON string: 204 response
issue_body:
Using flask as backend, it's impossible return anything but empty response along with status code 204. And [HTTP spec](https://www.w3.org/Protocols/rfc2616/rfc2616-sec10.html#sec10.2.5) explicitly says: 

> The 204 response MUST NOT include a message-body, and thus is always terminated by the first empty line after the header fields.

There is suggested solution retrieve data as text, but this seems like kludge and is ugly (IMHO). And I have number of endpoints in API with possible 204 response.

I believe jQuery / jQuery-Migrate should make exception for 204 status code and always return null for this case instead of trying parse response text.

Or I should report this in jQuery repo?

issue_title: pat-markspeciallinks does not allow the external_links_open_new_window behavior without also enabling mark_special_links
issue_body:
re: https://github.com/plone/mockup/pull/429

We made a mistake in converting the legacy mark_special_links javascript into the corresponding markspeciallinks pattern.  The name of the legacy script was a little misleading, in that it actually encapsulated two different behaviors which are actually independent from each other:  
* `external_links_open_new_window` and 
* `mark_special_links`
In other words, it should be possible to enable either one or the other, or both, from @@theming-controlpanel.
Instead, the way the pattern is implemented now, the `external_links_open_new_window` behavior can only be enabled iff the `mark_special_links` behavior is enabled.  This, in turn, is enabled simply by giving the pattern class to an HTML element.  Thus, without the pat-markspeciallinks class, it would be impossible to activate the `external_links_open_new_window` behavior by itself.

I propose to fix this by introducing an additional boolean option `mark_special_links`.

FYI: @agitator

issue_title: Clarify naming of Language independent folders
issue_body:
There's a lack of clarity on this feature in 2.x & master, mostly (IMO) because of the variety of names it has been given, including:
* Neutral root folder (title of the section in Readme.rst)
* language neutral folder (also mentioned in Readme.rst)
* language independent folder (the name of the FTI type, only visible in ZMI & code I think)
* lif (abbreviation of the above, which unfortunately clashes with "Language Independent Field", but fortunately only visible in code)
* Language shared (name of the folder according to Readme.rst)
* Media (*real* name of the folder in 2.x)
* "shared folder" (how it is referenced in translate menu)

So may I humbly suggest, 
1. the primary stakeholders decide on **one** name to use going forward?
2. can we change Readme.rst accordingly.

@bloodbare @saily @datakurre @sneridagh @jensens and anyone else?

It's not my place (as a minor contributor) to select which name should be used, but I've put a rewrite of the "Neutral root folder" section in Readme.rst as how I think best supposing that you selected "language neutral folder".   (Note I'm currently using 2.x, so tweaks *might* be required to the below for master (though I think not).  

> ## The "Media" folder - a shared "language neutral folder"

> The root language folders are used to house the content tree for the corresponding language.  However, there are some use cases where we need content that does not belong to any language. For example, for assets or side resources like images, videos and documents. For this reason PAM supplies a special **language neutral folder** to house these kind of objects. After PAM setup, there is a special folder called "Media", which can be accessed through the "Go to shared folder" item of the "Translate" menu. All items placed in this folder will have neutral as their default language and will be visible from the other root language folders as if they were placed there as well.  
**Note:** *Language Neutral Folder*'s have also been historically known as "Neutral root folder", "language independent folder" and "language shared (folder)"

issue_title: Strange behaviour with prometheus/alertmanager alert transitions
issue_body:
Here is important snippets from my configurations. Prometheus.rules:

```
ALERT alertName
    IF (metric > 1000) FOR 1m
    LABELS { severity="critical" }
    ANNOTATIONS {
    }

ALERT alertName
    IF (metric > 100) FOR 1m
    LABELS { severity="major" }
    ANNOTATIONS {
    }
```

Alertmanager.yml:

```
route:
  receiver:        'alerta'
  group_wait:      10s
  group_interval:  10s
  repeat_interval: 60s
  group_by:        ['alertname', 'host']

inhibit_rules:
- source_match:
    severity: 'critical'
  target_match:
    severity: 'major'
  equal: ['alertname', 'host']

receivers:
- name: 'alerta'
  webhook_configs:
  - url: 'http://localhost:8080/webhooks/prometheus?api-key=key'
    send_resolved: true
```

From alertmanager seems that all is working as expected, but there is a strange behaviour in Alerta.
 * If metric rises over 100, the major alarm is firing in alertmanager. Alerta receives the alert correctly.
 * If metric rises over 1000 , the critical alarm is firing in alertmanager and the major is inhibited. Alerta updates the alert correctly.
 * If metric falls under 1000 again (but is still over 100) alertmnager removes the critical one and the major one is firing again. But Alerta gets confused for some reason - number of duplicates is zeroed, it's sometimes unclear whether its major or critical now, and duplicate updates (different ID, but same datetime etc) will appear under alert data.

issue_title: Add method which returns proper envelope label
Do not pass details if the handler requires none
issue_body:
Hey, thank you for creating such a convenient library! I really like it and have no problems using it, but here's a small suggestion:

If a given handler does not make use of the `details` object, one has to either write:

```python
def default_handler(_: dict) -> None:
    '''Standard exception handling'''
    log(sys.exc_info()[1])
```
or:

```python
def default_handler(details: dict) -> None:
    '''Standard exception handling'''
    if details:
        pass
    log(sys.exc_info()[1])
```
in order to get a 10/10 result with pylint.

It would be better if you provided a way of not having to deal with this. Example:

```python
from inspect import signature

if len(signature(handler).parameters) < 1:
    def wrapper(_: dict) -> None:
        '''Wrapper for handlers that do not make use of details'''
        return handler()
    actual_handler = wrapper
else:
    actual_handler = handler 
```
So that this is possible:

```python
def default_handler() -> None:
    '''Standard exception handling'''
    log(sys.exc_info()[1])
```

Hi,

I was having all sorts of problems loading 3DS files and finally narrowed it down to sizeof( Discreet3DS::Chunk ) returning 8 when it should have been returning 6, ie: the PACK_STRUCT macro wasn't working.

A bit more research and it looks like using __attribute__ to control struct packing in mingw (which is what I'm using) is broken and has been for a long time! I'm using mingw-64 6.2.0 which is relatively recent and there are reports of this going back to 2012.

But it turns out that mingw supports #pragma pack too, slightly modified: #pragma pack(1) to enable packing, #pragma pack() to disable so I changed pushpack1.h and poppack1.h to use this instead and 3DS is now working! I recommend you guys change this in the repos too.

Bye,
Mark

Hi!

I see that ed25519 support is missing from PGPy. Since it is a commonly used curve in GPG, I started looking into how it could be implemented here.

It seems the first problem is that support for that curve is missing from OpenSSL itself, as shown by https://github.com/openssl/openssl/issues/487. According to https://github.com/openssl/openssl/issues/487#issuecomment-310086601, a patch was merged in june to add support in master, which should take some time to propagate everywhere. The two patches are https://github.com/openssl/openssl/pull/3585 and https://github.com/openssl/openssl/pull/3503 which seem to indicate the magic word may be `ED25519` but i'm not sure.

So I wonder if that could simply be implemented in PGPy's side with:

```patch
diff --git a/pgpy/_curves.py b/pgpy/_curves.py
index 9503075..8417d15 100644
--- a/pgpy/_curves.py
+++ b/pgpy/_curves.py
@@ -48,7 +48,13 @@ class BrainpoolP512R1(object):
     key_size = 512
 
 
+@utils.register_interface(ec.EllipticCurve)
+class Ed25519P256R1(object):
+    name = 'Ed25519'
+    key_size = 256
+
+
 # add these curves to the _CURVE_TYPES list
-for curve in [BrainpoolP256R1, BrainpoolP384R1, BrainpoolP512R1]:
+for curve in [BrainpoolP256R1, BrainpoolP384R1, BrainpoolP512R1, Ed25519P256R1]:
     if curve.name not in ec._CURVE_TYPES and curve.name in _openssl_get_supported_curves():
         ec._CURVE_TYPES[curve.name] = curve
```

would that make sense? or there are some fundamental things to change elsewhere in the code?

issue_title: It is impossible to catch a CrawlerRunningException
issue_body:
This code is used to trigger a crawler:

```python
def trigger_glue_crawler(access_key_id, access_key_secret):
    glue_client = boto3.client(
      "glue",
      aws_access_key_id=access_key_id,
      aws_secret_access_key=access_key_secret
    )
    response = glue_client.start_crawler(Name="example_crawler")
    print("Schema crawler started.")
```

But if the crawler is already running, this exception is thrown:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "example.py", line 102, in trigger_glue_crawler
    response = glue_client.start_crawler(Name="example_crawler")
  File "/lib/python3.6/site-packages/botocore/client.py", line 314, in _api_call
    return self._make_api_call(operation_name, kwargs)
  File "/lib/python3.6/site-packages/botocore/client.py", line 612, in _make_api_call
    raise error_class(parsed_response, operation_name)
botocore.errorfactory.CrawlerRunningException: An error occurred (CrawlerRunningException) when calling the StartCrawler operation: Crawler with name example_crawler has already started
```

Trying to catch the exception looks like this:

```python
def trigger_glue_crawler(access_key_id, access_key_secret):
  try:
    glue_client = boto3.client(
      "glue",
      aws_access_key_id=access_key_id,
      aws_secret_access_key=access_key_secret
    )
    response = glue_client.start_crawler(Name="example_crawler")
    print("Schema crawler started.")
  except botocore.errorfactory.CrawlerRunningException:
    print("Schema crawler already running.")
```

But that exception doesn't exist:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "example.py", line 121, in <module>
    trigger_glue_crawler(args.access_key_id, args.access_key_secret)
  File "example.py", line 105, in trigger_glue_crawler
    except botocore.errorfactory.CrawlerRunningException:
AttributeError: module 'botocore.errorfactory' has no attribute 'CrawlerRunningException'
```

That's not super surprising - I'm guessing that the exception class is dynamically generated based on a server response.  But it doesn't exist in `boto3.exceptions` either.  This snippet:

```python
def trigger_glue_crawler(access_key_id, access_key_secret):
  try:
    glue_client = boto3.client(
      "glue",
      aws_access_key_id=access_key_id,
      aws_secret_access_key=access_key_secret
    )
    response = glue_client.start_crawler(Name="example_crawler")
    print("Schema crawler started.")
  except boto3.exceptions.CrawlerRunningException:
    print("Schema crawler already running.")
```

results in the following error:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "example.py", line 120, in <module>
    trigger_glue_crawler(args.access_key_id, args.access_key_secret)
  File "example.py", line 104, in trigger_glue_crawler
    except boto3.exceptions.CrawlerRunningException:
AttributeError: module 'boto3.exceptions' has no attribute 'CrawlerRunningException'
```

As far as I can tell, it is impossible to specifically catch this exception.  But it's a pretty normal flow that I would like to tolerate with a different code path than other exceptions.

issue_title: s3.Bucket can't be used for cross-account buckets
issue_body:
The S3 API is weird in that you can't directly interrogate a bucket's creation date. The only place it appears is in ListBuckets output. So in #128 a custom loader was added for Bucket that reads through ListBuckets to grab this info. 

The problem is, ListBuckets only contains buckets owned by your current account. If you try to instantiate a `Bucket` with the name of bucket from a second account, the loader will 404, even if you've been granted cross-account permissions on that bucket.

I'd suggest that load() should opportunistically set the creation_date as is done now, but not fail if it can't be set. Then update the docs to not it will sometimes be None in cases where the bucket is owned by another account (or you lack ListBucket perms).

I'm using boto3-0.0.7. I'm expecting this code to output the sizes of the keys in the bucket:

s3 = boto3.resource('s3')
bucket = s3.Bucket(BUCKET)

issue_title: Support for special branch names in git submodule declarations
issue_body:
Git now supports the `branch` field in submodule declarations, and adds a twist that is not handled by GitPython.

**https://raw.githubusercontent.com/git/git/master/Documentation/RelNotes/2.10.2.txt**
 _* Recent git allows submodule.\<name\>.branch to use a special token
   "." instead of the branch name; the documentation has been updated
   to describe it._

**https://git-scm.com/docs/gitmodules**
_submodule.\<name\>.branch
A remote branch name for tracking updates in the upstream submodule. If the option is not specified, it defaults to master. A special value of . is used to indicate that the name of the branch in the submodule should be the same name as the current branch in the current repository. See the --remote documentation in git-submodule[1] for details._

When this issue is resolved, GitPython can handle special names and behaves similarly to cgit when encountering them.

Here's an important paragraph from http://aws.amazon.com/sdk-for-python/:

> Boto3 has two distinct levels of APIs. Client (or "low-level") APIs provide one-to-one mappings to the underlying HTTP API operations. Resource APIs hide explicit network calls but instead provide resource objects and collections to access attributes and perform actions.

The Mapbox Python SDK is by this measure, low level. Let's keep it low level and not mix in higher level abstractions. Not that higher level is bad, but it's another project or sub project.
