issue_title: R_HOME ignored when building package with long name (conda-build 2.0.11)
issue_body:
Building R packages with long names now fails. For example: 
```
$ conda build bioconductor-annotationfuncs
...
source tree in: /usr/prog/onc/lib/conda-channels/duraner1/bioconductor-annotationfuncs_1481279816278/work/AnnotationFuncs
+ source /usr/prog/onc/bin/miniconda3/bin/activate /usr/prog/onc/lib/conda-channels/duraner1/bioconductor-annotationfuncs_1481279816278/_b_env
+ mv DESCRIPTION DESCRIPTION.old
+ grep -v '^Priority: ' DESCRIPTION.old
+ /usr/prog/onc/lib/conda-channels/duraner1/bioconductor-annotationfuncs_1481279816278/_b_env/bin/R CMD INSTALL --build .
WARNING: ignoring environment value of R_HOME
/usr/prog/onc/lib/conda-channels/duraner1/bioconductor-annotationfuncs_1481279816278/_b_env/lib/R/bin/R: line 238: /opt/anaconda1anaconda2anaconda3/lib/R/etc/ldpaths: No such file or directory
Command failed: /bin/bash -x -e /usr/prog/onc/lib/conda-channels/duraner1/bioconductor-annotationfuncs_1481279816278/work/AnnotationFuncs/conda_build.sh
```

The error is that ldpaths is not correctly detected because R_HOME is ignored. 

Simply renaming the package to something shorter (e.g. bio-annotationfuncs) solves the issue. 

bioconductor-[package name] is the accepted name at the moment. 

Thanks.

issue_title: pip-installed numpy wheel not importable within conda environment
issue_body:
## Numpy failing in conda's root environment

1. Starting with Jessie Lite as a base image on a Raspberry Pi, I install miniconda3 and then I proceed to install pip with `conda install pip`. This is all done on the `root` environment of conda.

2. Next, I install the numpy wheel with the following command:
```
pip install numpy --extra-index-url https://www.piwheels.hostedpi.com/simple
```

3. Then, I try importing the numpy module inside the python (v3.4.3) interpreter and I get the following error:
```

Python 3.4.3 |Continuum Analytics, Inc.| (default, Aug 21 2015, 00:53:08)
Type 'copyright', 'credits' or 'license' for more information
IPython 6.2.1 -- An enhanced Interactive Python. Type '?' for help.

In [1]: import numpy
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
ImportError                               Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-1-d4cdadb62aa7> in <module>()
----> 1 import numpy

/opt/miniconda3/lib/python3.4/site-packages/numpy/__init__.py in <module>()
    164     from . import fft
    165     from . import polynomial
--> 166     from . import random
    167     from . import ctypeslib
    168     from . import ma

/opt/miniconda3/lib/python3.4/site-packages/numpy/random/__init__.py in <module>()
     97 with warnings.catch_warnings():
     98     warnings.filterwarnings("ignore", message="numpy.ndarray size changed")
---> 99     from .mtrand import *
    100
    101 # Some aliases:

ImportError: /opt/miniconda3/lib/python3.4/site-packages/numpy/random/mtrand.cpython-34m.so: undefined symbol: PyFPE_jbuf
```
I've done this multiple of times, so I'm fairly sure this is repeatable.

## Numpy imported within system-wide space

Next, I did something else, and that was to reflash the sd card with a fresh Jessie Lite again (we wouldn't want to have the environment contaminated at all) and install numpy system-wide with conda not installed.
Basically, I did the following things:
```
$ sudo apt-get install python3 python3-pip
$ sudo pip install numpy --extra-index-url https://www.piwheels.hostedpi.com/simple
```
Afterward, I launched the python interpreter and imported the numpy module, and it worked.

## Bottom Line

Bottom line is that on a Raspberry Pi, while in conda's environment, a pip-installed numpy module can't be imported whilst in the system-wide environment it works fine. The same wheel has been used for installing in both scenarios.

Now, you might ask yourself why haven't I used the `conda install numpy` command?
Well, first of all, keep in mind I'm developing a system for installing packages in offline mode (non interactive mode).

The issue is a lot of python packages can't be installed from continuum's repositories on a Raspberry Pi, because they are yet to be available. Because of this, I need to fill the void by installing packages with pip, but doing this creates another 2 problems:
1. Because I'm installing packages with pip and conda, there are moments when I get the infamous `Cannot remove entries from nonexistent file ***/site-pack
ages/easy-install.pth`  error, which can by the way, be avoided by adding the `--ignore-installed` option. Even with that, it makes me nervous thinking that errors like these can appear. And when updates are being done without a human's supervision, things can get ugly - so I can't trust that everything will go smoothly all the time.
2. A mechanism for installing/updating packages with conda and pip together is going to be inherently more complex than having just one tool. And since I'm only interested in installing python packages, pip seems to be the best choice, because there are a large number of packages. The only problem with the packages on PyPi is that for many of them, I don't get the wheels, but instead, the source code, which needs to be build. As an alternative to that, there is https://github.com/bennuttall/piwheels, which has pre-compiled wheels.

So as you can see, due to the lack of conda packages for python for the `armv7` architecture, I'm going to be stuck with pip and piwheels. I'm still going to use conda as an environment manager. To me, the conda + pip combo looks better than virtualenv + pip.

## TL;DR

Pip-installed numpy package can't be imported in conda's environment while being on a Raspberry Pi. Is there anyone else who's encountering this issue? Does anyone know what the problem is?

## Update

When pip installing from the source, it works (takes roughly 30 minutes on a Pi 3).

issue_title: how to modify conda env ps1 behavior
issue_body:
my current bash ps1 is as follows:

```
bldred='\e[1;31m' # Red
bldcyn='\e[1;36m' # Cyan
bldwht='\e[1;37m' # White
txtrst='\e[0m'    # Text Reset - Useful for avoiding color bleed

export PS1="\n\[$bldred\]\u\[$txtrst\]@\[$bldwht\]\h\[$txtrst\]:\[$bldcyn\]\w\[$txtrst\]$ "
```

However, running:
```
source activate <env-name-here>
```

by default, tells conda to prepend the env-name to my PS1:

(<env-name-here>)
user@short-domain:fullpath$

issue_title: query_position returns a None when is_del
issue_body:
Hey all,

This is actually more a discussion point then a real issue, but could not find a discussion board. Apologies if it is misplaced.
However within the last version (0.8.3) you changed the old behaviour to return the position before a deletion within an aligned read as the query_position when the query contains a deletion at that position. It now returns a None. That sounds logical, because the reference position is not present in the read, so there is no query position for it, that is the whole idea of a deletion. However, this introduces a problem as well. For a particular PileupRead, I cannot retrieve any information any more where I am within this particular read. For example I have no clue any longer if my deletion is at the border of a read (and as such less trustworthy) or right in the middle. 
So I personally think this is a loss of function, and would propose to reintroduce the old behaviour. Also because a deletion itself has a position: it is located between two bases. That is a bit problematic, because you do not want to return 2 numbers (and returning a position and a half is an ugly solution), however the old behaviour was following the vcf style of referencing to the position of a deletion by referencing to the position of the base before. So I think the old solution was the best and most elegant to indicate the position of deletion within a PileupRead.

Kind regards, 
Bart

Hi,
I'm counting the substitutions on a sam/bam file, but I'm getting 2 different results depending on the strategy.

Count the subs. by counting the ACGT on MD tags.

```python
sam = pysam.AlignmentFile(sam_file, "rb")
for read in sam:
    if any('MD' == tg[0] for tg in read.get_tags()):
        md = read.get_tag('MD')
            for s in md:
                f s in "ACGT":
                    mismatches += 1
```

Pileup
```python
# iterate over each base on the reference
for pileup_col in sam.pileup():
    # get the reference base
    refbase = fasta.fetch(reference=pileup_col.reference_name,
                          start=pileup_col.reference_pos,
                          end=pileup_col.reference_pos + 1).upper()

    for pileup_read in pileup_col.pileups:
        if not pileup_read.is_del and not pileup_read.is_refskip:
            if pileup_read.alignment.query_sequence[pileup_read.query_position] != refbase:
                mismatches += 1
```

Using get_aligned_pairs
```python
sam = pysam.AlignmentFile(sam_file, "rb")
for read in sam:
    aligned_positions = read.get_aligned_pairs(with_seq=True)
    for read_pos, reference_pos, base in aligned_positions:
            # lower bases represent a substitution
            if base is not None and base in "acgt":
                 mismatches += 1
```

The issue is that counting the ACGT in the MD tags results in ~4 times more substitutions that the other 2 methods. Any help is appreciated. 

Thanks

issue_title: Curator is very slow on cluster with many indices
issue_body:
We have a cluster with about 7000 indices, with new indices being created every day. We would like to run curator daily to assign indices to different nodes (SSD vs spinning disk), and adjust their replica settings, depending on the age of the index. However, when operating on a large number of indices (e.g. --older-than 90 days yields thousands of indices on our cluster), we find that curator spends a long time querying each individual index, checking it's status. Using --debug, I noticed that curator queries e.g. /_cat/indices/my-index-20151202?h=status&format=json for each index, which can 5 or even 10 seconds on our cluster. This means that a single execution can take 10 or more hours to complete, and we would like to run it more often than that.

Digging into the code, I've found this:

 ```python
    # This workaround using the _cat API is for AWS Elasticsearch, since it does
    # not allow users to poll the cluster state.  It may also be faster than
    # using the cluster state.
    if get_version(client) >= (1, 5, 0):
        indices = client.cat.indices(index=index_name, format='json', h='status')
        # This workaround is also for AWS, as the _cat API still returns text/plain
        # even with ``format='json'``.
        if not isinstance(indices, list):  # content-type: text/plain
            indices = json.loads(indices)
        (index_info,) = indices
        return index_info['status'] == 'close'
    else:
        index_metadata = client.cluster.state(
            index=index_name,
            metric='metadata',
        )
        return index_metadata['metadata']['indices'][index_name]['state'] == 'close'
```

I suppose this decision to query each index rather than using the cluster state is causing this slowness for us.

Is there a compromise that can be made, such as adding a --use-cluster-state (I'm sure a better name can be imagined) option?

Thanks.

issue_title: Document timestring regular expressions and how dates are matched in more detail
issue_body:
Resulting from https://github.com/elastic/curator/issues/707, we should document in more detail how the regular expressions resulting from the timestring's look and how dates are matched.

E.g.` %Y.%W` translates into `^.*\d{4}\.\d{2}.*$ ` and thus could also match daily indices.

```
$ curator show snapshots --repository XXXXX --newer-than 4 --time-unit hours
2015-08-13 10:14:49,159 INFO      Job starting: show snapshots
2015-08-13 10:15:19,201 WARNING   No snapshots matched provided args.
ERROR. No snapshots matched provided args.
$ echo $?
0
```

It's ether and error or not an error and the exit code should reflect it.
Being a command line tool, it will be useful if no matched snapshot results in non-zero exit code.

Hi

It looks like this parameter was removed in https://github.com/elastic/elasticsearch-py/commit/481dc907ad60e7e88bd16697e0e2c92ac56b99c2. While the entire [Delete By Query](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/reference/current/docs-delete-by-query.html) API was deprecated in `1.5.3`, it's still being used in our code base. Not sure why this specific parameter was removed as there's no indication that it's no longer valid (deprecation aside) and still necessary where issuing a `DELETE` request with a body is not possible (e.g. Google AppEngine).

from elasticsearch import Elasticsearch
es = Elasticsearch(['http://localhost:9200'])

issue_title: What does upgrade option mean on the azure portal?

Hi, Lets say I have a VMSS which is running 4 VMs and I deployed them using ARM template.
now I made some changes to cloud init files or some other files which are used by the VMSS. Here there are two things which I can do
1) I can delete the whole resource group and redeploy the updated ARM Templates which is like fresh installs the VMSS from scratch.which is good.
2) I can use two options which I can see the portal. first upgrade the VMs to latest model and after that I can do reimage which upgrades the VMS to the latest image.

This works only after pushing the latest changes and upgrading the VMs to latest model and then reimage.
Might be tricky but there is no proper documentation I can find.
I don't see any az commands which can be used to do both upgrade and reimage using the command line. I can see the upgrade but it is kind of tricky and not sure what does the rolling upgrade do which is mentioned in the commands in the above document.

issue_title: [Storage] File copy source convenience parameters don't work
Bash process substitution not working for az group deployment create
issue_body:
### Description
Hi,
Looks like the azure cli does not allow to load files from `named pipes`, a good example would be using [process substitution](http://wiki.bash-hackers.org/syntax/expansion/proc_subst) technique to produce the file through a command, like rendering a template with some external tool to then feed az-cli with it...

A trivial example to show the issue
```
$ az group deployment create \
    --name="mydeployment" \
    --resource-group="myresourcegroup" \
    --template-file=<( cat template.json ) \
    --parameters=<( cat parameters.json )
```
Will cause something like
```
Unable to parse parameter: /dev/fd/62
```
I've tracked the issue in the code to [here](https://github.com/Azure/azure-cli/blob/azure-cli-vm-2.0.9/src/command_modules/azure-cli-resource/azure/cli/command_modules/resource/_validators.py#L44)

As you can see the tool is only looking for regular files, but in the example I've provided a named pipe is given instead... This would be really awesome to have the possibility of reading from FIFO files so process substitution can be used with it.

I'd be really happy to create the PR myself once agreed on the approach, I was thinking on simply checking if isfile() for any OS or if OS linux if isfile() or isFifo() using something like

```
 import stat, os
 stat.S_ISFIFO(os.stat(path).st_mode)
```
This is also true that this functionality might be implemented for any parameter that should be a filein the whole scope of az-cli tool so further digging would be necessary ...

Let me know what you think about
---
### Environment
azure-cli-vm-2.0.9
Ubuntu 16.04.2TLS
GNU bash, version 4.3.48(1)-release

Thanks!

issue_title: sfctl should validate that it is compatible with Service Fabric runtime on cluster
issue_body:
Connection to a cluster with Fabric runtime that is not supported by installed version of sfctl succeeds but almost none of the other commands work.

Expected behavior:
sfctl should check the version of runtime in sfctl cluster select and if it cannot support the version of Fabric Runtime deployed to the cluster it should give an actionable error.

I am using DeploymentsOperations.create_or_update() to deploy resources and using AzureOperationPoller.result( ) to get the result every 30 seconds:

```python
result = async_poller.result(timeout=30)
logger.info('Deploy %s provisioning_state: %s', result.name, result.properties.provisioning_state)
```
however, I found the **result** variable sometimes have unexpected content
So I used try catch for it:
```python
try:
  result = async_poller.result(timeout=30)
  logger.info('Deploy %s provisioning_state: %s', result.name, result.properties.provisioning_state)
except AttributeError as e:
  # result variable may not have correct attribute we want
  logger.warning('Getting AttributeError: %s', e)
  logger.debug('result: %s', result)
  logger.debug('response: %s', async_poller._response)
```

here is the log output:
```
2017-02-20 05:27:40 WARNING - Getting AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'provisioning_state'
2017-02-20 05:27:40 DEBUG - result: {'id': None, 'name': None, 'properties': None}
2017-02-20 05:27:40 DEBUG - response: <Response [200]>
```
Some questions about this behavior:
1. From the REST API document: https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/azure-resource-manager/resource-manager-async-operations
   HTTP return code 200 means finished, but before deployment is finished, return code is always 200, is it correct?

2. why the **result** variable contains None type of id/name/properties?

When I call `create_blob_from_bytes` with a `bytes` object, I see the following warning in the console (this is against the storage emulator, using version 0.32.0):

```
sys:1: ResourceWarning: unclosed <socket.socket fd=1060, family=AddressFamily.AF_INET, type=SocketKind.SOCK_STREAM, proto=0, laddr=('127.0.0.1', 55469), raddr=('127.0.0.1', 10000)>

    * `100000000 - 54681856 = 45318144`
    * `4294903296 - 4249585152 = 45318144`

I can also provide you with the other logs of the successfully uploaded blocks, etc., but they contain little information:
Content-length is consistent, return-codes are ok (201), timestamps are reasonable, blocklist/head upload happens in the correct order, etc.

I can not reliably reproduce the error, but I see it occasionally when uploading several TiB from on-premise as well as Azure-VMs.

### Have you found a mitigation/solution?
Not yet, but complete re-upload of the block *after* the blocklist has been committed seems sensible.

hi @asottile 

So I'm wondering if we could make it easier to get started with pre-commit, if you're just writing a JS library and don't already have a venv for anything else.

I've written a proof of concept and I'd love to get your thoughts - https://github.com/magicmark/pre-commit-wrapper-npm

This is an npm package which just wraps pre-commit, and makes a venv during the `install` hook.

Current Drawbacks:
- Because I'm not sourcing the auto-created venv, `pre-commit` could refer to some globally installed pre-commit instead, causing version conflict issues (I ran into this). Wondering if we can somehow make the `.git/hooks/pre-commit` always refer to the correct executable?
- If there isn't already a `.pre-commit-config.yaml`, the wrapper package install fails when doing `install-hooks`. This can be improved though.
- Any more that I've missed?

Pros:
- Reduction of boilerplate (no need to add venv creation to makefiles, or aactivator)
- Easier to get started with pre-commit in a js, non-python package

lmk what your take on this is. thanks!

issue_title: Querying aiohttp test_server with third party clients freezes
issue_body:
## Long story short

Hi all! 

I am trying to set up some tests that would query aiohttp test server with a 3rd party client (bravado in my case).

The example code would look something like this:

```
@pytest.fixture()
async def server(aiohttp_server) -> TestServer:
    app = create_app()
    server = await aiohttp_server(app, port=TEST_PORT)
    return server

async def test_get_all_bravado(self, server, headers):
    await server.start_server()
    response = swagger_client_sync.entity.getAll(
        _request_options={"headers": headers}).result()
    await server.close()
    assert response == []
```
## Actual behaviour

When I try to run this test, the execution freezes (pytest command execution never finishes).
The behavior is the same if I use another 3rd party client to query the test server, for example, `requests`.
If I use the aiohttp test client,  the test runs correctly.

## Your environment

aiohttp==3.0.5
asynctest==0.12.0
pytest==3.4.1
pytest-aiohttp==0.3.0

## Long story short

Related to the comments in #1219 

I made some PR here and at first I didn't manage to make the tests work properly. Recently I investigated a little bit more and I realized that tox was installing its own version of py.test and somehow ignores any py.test plugins I installed on the system.

## Expected behaviour

Running tox should make the test run out of the box.

## Actual behaviour

```
Fixture 'mocker' not found
```

## Steps to reproduce

Hard to tell... I have a different version of py.test installed and tox is installing its own probably because the version is different but maybe it would do it anyway.

## Your environment

Arch Linux, Python 3.6, fish...

## A possible solution

```patch
diff --git a/tox.ini b/tox.ini
index fd0a550e..02dcec44 100644
--- a/tox.ini
+++ b/tox.ini
@@ -1,11 +1,12 @@
 [tox]
 
 envlist = check, {py34,py35}-{debug,release}-{cchardet,cython,pure}, report
 
 [testenv]
 
 deps =
     pytest
+    pytest-mock
     # pytest-cov
     coverage
     gunicorn
```

I just added pytest-mock to the dependencies to make sure it's installed in the tox environment.

issue_title: Connection pooling seems not to work
issue_body:
## Long story short

I'm writing a http downloader demo and I want to download multiple parts (e.g. 4 parts) at the same time. I did set the parameter limit_per_host=4 as the document, but the program kept creating new socket constantly. 

## Expected behaviour
Downloading 4 parts of the file at the same time, and then reuse the connections for next 4 parts.

## Actual behaviour
The program kept creating new connections, and I found no connections put back to the connection pool. 
From the pics below you can see that the script created a lot of connections.
![2](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/15943189/32036512-f566fcb0-ba52-11e7-99a4-c8af5cd120a7.PNG)
![3](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/15943189/32036520-fb7e2182-ba52-11e7-8f32-70bf89e69e28.PNG)


## More details
I find ResponseHandler.should_close always returns True, which cause TCPConnector._release() will never put the connection into its connection pool.

## Steps to reproduce
Here is my script.

```Python
from aiohttp import connector, ClientSession, hdrs
from math import ceil
import asyncio

DEFAULT_MAX_CONNECTION_PER_HOST = 4
DEFAULT_CHUNK_SIZE = 262144
TEST_LINK = r"https://dldir1.qq.com/qqfile/qq/QQ8.9.5/22062/QQ8.9.5.exe"

async def test_download():
    con = connector.TCPConnector(limit_per_host=DEFAULT_MAX_CONNECTION_PER_HOST)
    async with ClientSession(connector=con) as session:
        async with session.head(TEST_LINK, headers={hdrs.CONNECTION: hdrs.KEEP_ALIVE}) as response:
            total_length = int(response.headers["Content-Length"])

            # Calculating the start and the end index of each chunk
            chunks = [(start * DEFAULT_CHUNK_SIZE, end * DEFAULT_CHUNK_SIZE - 1)for start, end in zip(tuple(range(int(ceil(total_length / DEFAULT_CHUNK_SIZE)) + 1)[:-1]),
                        tuple(range(int(ceil(total_length / DEFAULT_CHUNK_SIZE)) + 1))[1:])]

        for i in chunks:
            async with session.get(TEST_LINK, headers={hdrs.CONNECTION: hdrs.KEEP_ALIVE,
                                                   "Range": "{0}-{1}".format(*i)}) as response:
                print(response.status, i)




if __name__ == "__main__":
    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
    loop.run_until_complete(test_download())
```

## Your environment

Windows 7 64-bit, Python 3.6.3

issue_title: How to correctly escape values should be better documented
issue_body:
Hello

I was trying to assist someone using aiomysql for the first time. I myself use postgre and asyncpg, so I was relying on the documentation while helping them.

But the examples in the documentation use hard-coded queries or weird ways to escape, something a regular user would never do. Also the use of variables is too heavy. Examples should be clear, every argument of execute should be self evident, I think having the need to look at an additional variables while reading the documentation is unnecessary. 

Examples:
* First SELECT in asyncpg docs: ``row = await conn.fetchrow('SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = $1', 'Bob')``. Clearly uses escaping, no weird variables.
* First SELECT in aiomysql docs: ``await cur.execute("SELECT Host,User FROM user")``. Doesn't escape anything
* First escaping SELECT in aiomysql docs: No select query is escaping python types...
* First escaping INSERT in aiomysql docs: ``await cur.executemany("INSERT INTO music_style (id, name) values (%s,%s)", data)``. Uses variable data that I would have to look up what it is...

issue_title: Make Page Up and Page Down work consistently
issue_body:
Notebooks have quite a complex cursor model, with focus being possible on a cell or within a cell, and different semantics for keyboard and mouse navigation events like cursor up, down, page up, page down, and mouse scrolling.  Mostly this is reasonable, if a little tricky for users to learn at first.

But it seems that there are a couple of places where the bindings for Page Up / Page Down either fail to allow easy navigation around the document as a whole, or fail to work at all. Two specific examples:

* If a user enters a code cell, hits escape, and then hits page up / down, no scrolling happens. This is clearly a bug. (I'm seeing this in Debian's `python-notebook` package, version 4.2.3).
* If a user enters a code cell, page up and page down will only scroll to the top and bottom of that cell. Though this is sort of consistent with the notebook focus model, I find it's really annoying and makes looking around a notebook much harder. Certainly Page Up / Page Down at the end of a cell should take the browser window to any part of the notebook. I'm not sure, and its probably an empirical user research question, whether it should also break the focus out of that cell, leave it there, or jump it to other cells.

issue_title: Shortcut Key in File Editor to Block Comment
issue_body: Many text editors (see for example [Sublime](https://stackoverflow.com/a/11443903/2638485)) have a shortcut key that block comments the selected lines of code. Given the file editor has as language selection, this language can be used to properly block comment. ie in Python this would just be prepending each line in the selection with a `#`.

issue_title: check website is a valid URI
issue_body:
pypi checks the url key is a valid uri according to rfc3986, hence addons with an invalid website key in the manifest cannot be published to pypi.

Hence, I'm suggesting the addition of the following check to pylint-odoo:

```python
import rfc3986

website = manifest.get('website')
uri = rfc3986.uri_reference(website)
if not uri.is_valid(require_scheme=True, require_authority=True):
    # error
if not if uri.scheme not in {"http", "https"}:
    # error
```

Changes have been made to simplify setup:
https://github.com/Duke-Translational-Bioinformatics/duke-data-service/issues/622

issue_title: Need to take into account automatic paging that can happen with big folders.
issue_body: The API has a limit on the number of items it returns and you must make several more requests to gather all pages to get the entire list. Right now the python code assumes it will get the entire list back at once.

It seems that `autograd.numpy.kron` cannot handle 1-dimensional input arrays. This following example works where the inputs are made 2-dimensional
```
import autograd.numpy as np
from autograd import grad
x_test = np.array([1., 2., 3., 4.])
obj_fun = lambda x: np.sum(np.kron(np.atleast_2d(x[:2]),np.atleast_2d(x[2:])))
g = grad(obj_fun)
print g(x_test)
```
however if the inputs are 1D then an error is raised
```
obj_fun = lambda x: np.sum(np.kron(x[:2],x[2:]))
g = grad(obj_fun)
print g(x_test)
```
Output:
```
autograd/numpy/numpy_grads.pyc in <lambda>(blockrow)
    169 def grad_kron(argnum, G, ans, vs, gvs, A, B):
    170     def blocks(G):
--> 171         return map(lambda blockrow: anp.split(blockrow, A.shape[1], 1),
    172                                     anp.split(G,        A.shape[0], 0))
    173     flat = lambda lst: [item for sublist in lst for item in sublist]

IndexError: tuple index out of range
```

issue_title: Staging directory can miss helper scripts etc
issue_body:
e.g. https://github.com/peterjc/galaxy_mira/issues/1 where the tool folder includes multiple files, including:

- ``mira4_de_novo.xml`` <- one of the tool definitions
- ``mira4.py`` <-- wrapper script called in XML file, appears to be staged
- ``mira4_make_bam.py`` <-- imported from wrapper script, not staged

On that issue @jmchilton wrote:

*Pulsar doesn't know how to see the required files that need to be transferred - tool authors need to be able to mark these up in the tool XML but it is currently not implemented (galaxyproject/galaxy#1609). This is urgent and is something I will do this summer I think - but probably not until after the ISMB.*

I think in the short term it would be safer to default to staging *all* the files next to the tool XML, as historically Galaxy tool authors have been able to assume they will be present in the same folder.

issue_title: Write a manuscript for BioConda and BioContainers on Computational Platforms
issue_body:
## BioConda and BioContainers - A Ubiquitous, Lightweight Approach for Reproducible Bioinformatics

### Outline

The problems associated with reproducibility of web services has been well documented [citation needed], so reproducible informatics requires reproducible software installation. There are many approaches to reproducible software installation for informatics - DebianMed, Docker, EasyInstall, software modules, and so on. Many work great in cloud and container environments - where the researcher has full control of a virtual machine or container and may choose whatever software installation mechanism makes sense, but are less appropriate at high performance computing (HPC) centers where large centralized resources make such freedom impossible. On the other hand, the HPC centric approaches don't provide options such as ready-to-run software containers ideal for the cloud. Futhermore, some approaches are built to work with command-line scripting while others are built for specific computational platforms such as the Galaxy Tool Shed or Dockstore. This paper outlines an approach that fits all of these scenarios. Combing a popular binary package manager containing thousands of bioinformatics and general purpose scientific software recipes and container environments built using these binaries for various technologies such as Docker, rtk, and Singularity and then making these packages and containers available on command-line and inside computational platforms such as Galaxy and workflow platforms such as Toil we believe we provide a pervasive approach to reproducibility that works regardless of the technologies used.

We will recreate the analysis in PAPER X four different ways. Using customized shell scripts at an HPC facility, an IPython notebook on a Mac OS X laptop, in a Galaxy history on usegalaxy.org, and deployed on Amazon EC 2 with Toil using the Common Workflow Language. We will use the same binaries seamlessly across these environments leveraging the technologies that make the most sense for each environment.

Would it be possible to add a setup script so installation of this module from pip is possible? 

Many thanks!

issue_title: Duplicate column titles/names will only return one of the columns
issue_body:
This is a corner case and I'm open to a "won't fix", but came across this and didn't expect the behaviour today: two examples where selecting columns that render out as having the same name will silently squash one of them in output:

## Example 1 - Titles:

```python
import giraffez

table_name = 'schema.tester'

drop_sql = f"DROP TABLE {table_name}"
create_sql = f"""CREATE MULTISET TABLE {table_name} (
    field_1 DECIMAL(18,0) TITLE 'Number',
    field_2 DECIMAL(12,0) TITLE 'Number')"""
insert_sql = f"INSERT INTO {table_name} SELECT 1 AS field_1, 2 AS field_2"
read_sql = f"SELECT field_1, field_2 FROM {table_name}"

with giraffez.Cmd() as cmd:
    if cmd.exists(table_name):
        cmd.execute(drop_sql)
    cmd.execute(create_sql)
    cmd.execute(insert_sql)
    res = list(cmd.execute(read_sql).to_dict())
    print(res)
    cmd.execute(drop_sql)
```

This code results in `[{'number': 2.0}]`, because `Column.name` uses `self.title` over `self.original_name` ([here](https://github.com/capitalone/giraffez/blob/master/giraffez/types.py#L111-L115)).  Note, if you BulkExport out the data, the export.to_str() still works fine, but the to_dict() will overwrite (because it's a dict)

## Example 2 - duplicate column names in multiple tables:
```python
create_a = "CREATE MULTISET VOLATILE TABLE tbl_a AS (SELECT 1 AS fld_a) WITH DATA ON COMMIT PRESERVE ROWS"
create_b = "CREATE MULTISET VOLATILE TABLE tbl_b AS (SELECT 1 AS fld_a) WITH DATA ON COMMIT PRESERVE ROWS"
read_sql = "SELECT tbl_a.fld_a, tbl_b.fld_a FROM tbl_a INNER JOIN tbl_b ON tbl_a.fld_a = tbl_b.fld_a"

with giraffez.Cmd() as cmd:
    cmd.execute(create_a)
    cmd.execute(create_b)
    res = cmd.execute(read_sql)
    for row in res:
        print(row)
```

This code results in `{'fld_a': 1}`.

If you select the same field name in one query/table (e.g. `SELECT 1 AS field_1, 2 AS field_1`), Teradata will error out with 3863: Duplicate definition of field.

Wondering if giraffez should exception out when there are duplicates in the list of column names?  Or maybe just when calling `to_dict()` or `to_list()` on Cmd or Export, which would produce unexpected results?  (especially if you're exporting, the fields will still be in `export.columns.names` but would have fewer records in `to_dict() items`).  Both of these could be fixed by the user providing an alias, but as a user I'd like giraffez to be "noisier" if there was going to be an issue.

issue_title: DataCollector: store datasets internally as structured arrays
issue_body:
At the moment ``DataCollector`` keeps a dictionary for each of:
* model_vars
* agent_vars
* tables

These currently store values that are lists of tuples, lists of lists of tuples, or dictionaries of lists. This works fine for small models, but performance in using these data or making ``pandas.DataFrame``s from them doesn't scale up well to thousands of agents over thousands of steps.

One option is to use [structured arrays](http://docs.scipy.org/doc/numpy/user/basics.rec.html). These require knowing in advance the size of the array you need to make them worthwhile, but even for models in which agents appear and disappear they could still be used effectively at least at the level of individual steps.

issue_title: Injecting settings changes into test nodes using hooks
issue_body:
I'm attempting to modify some basic settings within a manager class I maintain by using hooks.

This has been working great for a long time as long as I'm running without xdist. I import my settings class, make my changes and the changes are seen within my test methods. 

However, as soon as I attempt this same pattern using xdist hooks, this trick fails to work. The test nodes are unaware of my changes to the global scope.

Example:
```python
# test-settings-injection.py
import pytest

from settings import base as settings_base


# Tests
def test_foo():
    assert settings_base.settings.IMPORTANT_SETTING == 'parallel value'


def test_foo2():
    assert settings_base.settings.IMPORTANT_SETTING == 'parallel value'


@pytest.mark.serial
def test_bar():
    assert settings_base.settings.IMPORTANT_SETTING == 'serial value'


# Execute Tests
def main():
    class MyPlugin(object):
        def __init__(self):
            self.__name__ = 'myplugin'
            self.collected = set()

        def pytest_xdist_node_collection_finished(self, node, ids):
            self.collected.update(set(ids))

        def pytest_collection_modifyitems(self, items):
            for item in items:
                self.collected.update({item.nodeid})

        def pytest_configure(self, config):
            # mutate setting value
            from settings import base as settings_base
            settings_base.settings.IMPORTANT_SETTING = 'serial value'

        def pytest_testnodeready(self, node):
            # mutate setting value
            from settings import base as settings_base
            settings_base.settings.IMPORTANT_SETTING = 'parallel value'

    # this works
    myplugin = MyPlugin()
    pytest.main(['test-settings-injection.py', '-s', '-m', 'serial'], plugins=[myplugin])
    print('Serial Collected:', myplugin.collected)

    # this doesn't work
    myplugin = MyPlugin()
    pytest.main(['test-settings-injection.py', '-s', '-m', 'not serial', '-d', '--tx', '{}*popen//python=python3'.format(2), ], plugins=[myplugin])
    print('Parallel (threads) Collected:', myplugin.collected)

    # this doesn't work
    myplugin = MyPlugin()
    pytest.main(['test-settings-injection.py', '-s', '-m', 'not serial', '-n2', ], plugins=[myplugin])
    print('Parallel (CPUs) Collected:', myplugin.collected)


if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()
```

Is there anyway to do this sort of patching within the non-master test nodes?


Versions:
```
pytest==3.6.2
pytest-xdist==1.22.2
```

Test project here: https://github.com/westhomas/pytest-testapp
Run with `python test-settings-injection.py`

Description
===========
[KIP-82](https://cwiki.apache.org/confluence/display/KAFKA/KIP-82+-+Add+Record+Headers) adds support for message headers. The librdkafka support is in a PR here https://github.com/edenhill/librdkafka/pull/1480

I wanted to track python support here and discuss the python api when it lands upstream.

The headers are an ordered multimap with `string key -> binary value` where a key can be repeated.

There are a couple ways to support this in producer and message api.

List of Tuples/Lists:
```
headers_to_send = [('key1', b'value1'), ('key2', b'value2'), ('key1', b'repeated_key')]
producer.produce('topic_name', b'message_value', headers=headers_to_send)
```

Ordered dict. Python doesn't have a multimap built in, but we could build one or just use an ordered map where values are always nested in lists.
```
headers_to_send = OrderedMultiDict()
headers_to_send['key1'] = [b'value1',  b'repeated_key']
headers_to_send['key2'] = [b'value1']

OR

headers_to_send = OrderedMultiDict()
headers_to_send['key1'] = b'value1'
headers_to_send['key1'].append(b'repeated_key')
headers_to_send['key2'] = b'value1'

producer.produce('topic_name', b'message_value', headers=headers_to_send)
```

What ever the api for producer the consumer `msg.headers()` should return the same format as the producer input. Also for multi-version kafka support mirror the `msg.timestamp()` return flag of `(int, X)` could be used.
```
msg = consumer.poll()
supported_flag, list_of_tuples = msg.headers()
print(list_of_tuples)  # [('key1', b'value1'), ('key2', b'value2'), ('key1', b'repeated_key')]
```

This isn't an exhaustive list of options but i wanted to get the discussion started.

issue_title: Reset weights in a less hacky range
Do not automagically switch from system-wide to --user install of the kernelspec
issue_body:
I find the automatic switch from system-wide to --user installs in `python -mmatlab_kernel.install` rather annoying.  At least there should be a way to force a sys-wide install and error out if you don't have the permissions, e.g.
- no flag means the current behavior ("try system-wide, default to user if fails")
- `--user` means user install
- `--no-user` (or `--system`) means only system-wide.

In practice I think that the correct way to provide for kernelspec installation may be to have a command that *dumps* a kernelspec folder to a temporary place (printed on stdout) and rely on `jupyter kernelspec install` directly, e.g.
```
$ jupyter kernelspec install $(python -mmatlab_kernel.dump_kernelspec)
```
(possibly with a (yet to be implemented) flag to jupyter to clean up the folder afterwards).  This would directly offer any ability that `jupyter kernelspec install` may gain, e.g. installation to arbitrary prefixes (jupyter/jupyter_client#42, jupyter/jupyter_client#156).
Thoughts?

issue_title: Setup builds so xfail doesn't count towards code coverage
issue_body:
We've lately added quite a few `xfail` tests as a sort of "to do", but I realized that this may cause the `codecov` metrics to be less meaningful than one might hope. The reason being that `xfail` tests can fail for *any* reason, including that they went down some random buggy code path whose *proper* behavior is not actually covered, and that buggy code path threw an actual bug we should be aware of. As such, I'd rather code executed as the result of `xfail` tests *not* contribute to the code coverage. (They should still be *run* because I'd like to know if they turn from xfail to xpass).

If there's not just some standard `pytest-coverage` or `coverage` option that does this, I think it's simple enough to set up by just running `pytest` with all `xfail` tests skipped with `coverage` on, then running it a second time with coverage off and *only* `xfail` tests included.

If `None` is passed to the parser, I would expect either a `ValueError` or a `TypeError` to be raised, but instead `AttributeError` is raised because it tries to call `read` on the object. With `datetime.datetime.strptime`, if you pass it anything other than a string type, it raises a `TypeError`. I think we may want to do something similar. Certainly raising `AttributeError` violates principle of least surprise.

Since [timelex](https://github.com/dateutil/dateutil/blob/master/dateutil/parser.py#L67) is already using explicit type checks, we may want to change those two `if` statements to an `if`, `elif`, `else` triplet. I'm also open to possible solutions that would use duck typing.

```python
from dateutil.parser import parse
parse(None)
```

 ...

     81             else:
---> 82                 nextchar = self.instream.read(1)
     83                 while nextchar == '\x00':
     84                     nextchar = self.instream.read(1)

AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'read'
```

This was with Python3.4, while Python3.5 works just fine. Telethon 0.13.6 (0f10e14) works fine as well. The actual file/line is `telethon/tl/types/__init__.py`:

    return b''.join((b':\x1d\xe8\x98',struct.pack('<i', self.user_id),TLObject.serialize_string(self.description),b'\x15\xc4\xb5\x1c',struct.pack('<i', len(self.commands)),*[(x.to_bytes()) for x in self.commands],))

And if I researched correctly, then the issue is that [PEP 448](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0448/) is not supported in Python3.4.

issue_title: Installation broken on Fedora because other lib structures
issue_body:
On fedora after installing libdwarf-devel (and other from family), libdwarf.h and dwarf.h are located in `/usr/include/libdwarf` not in `/usr/include/`, so the installation crashes:
`src/hotpatch/elf.c:9:19: fatal error: dwarf.h: No such file or directory`

After copying both files level up, then it crashes:
```src/hotpatch/elf.c:15:25: fatal error: libelf/gelf.h: No such file or directory```
`gelf.h` is NOT in subdirectory, copying file to `libelf` subdir solves the problem.


below are packages contents

/usr/include/libdwarf/dwarf.h
/usr/include/libdwarf/libdwarf.h

issue_title: `missing` and `default` values not handled as documented
issue_body:
Hi, 

**given following schema:**

~~~python
from datetime import datetime
from marshmallow import Schema, fields


class Foo(object):
    def __init__(self, created_at=None):
        self.created_at = created_at


class FooSchema(Schema):
    FOO_TIME_FORMAT = '%Y%m%d%H%M%S'
    
    created_at = fields.LocalDateTime(
        format=FOO_TIME_FORMAT,
        default=lambda: datetime(2017, 5, 4, 3, 2, 1), 
        missing=lambda: datetime(2017, 9, 8, 7, 6, 5),
        required=False, allow_none=False
    )


schema = FooSchema()
~~~

**I get:**

~~~python
json_str, dumps_errors = schema.dumps(Foo())
json_str
# => '{"created_at": null}'
data, loads_errors = schema.loads('{}')
data
# => {}
~~~

**I would expect it to work as following**

(https://marshmallow.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api_reference.html#marshmallow.fields.Field):

~~~python
json_str, dumps_errors = schema.dumps(Foo())
json_str
# => '{"created_at": "20170504030201"}'
data, loads_errors = schema.loads('{}')
data
# => {'created_at': datetime.datetime(2017, 9, 8, 7, 6, 5)}
~~~

**I also tried to apply workaround from #378, but it gets me the same result:**

~~~python
class FooSchema(Schema):
    FOO_TIME_FORMAT = '%Y%m%d%H%M%S'
    
    created_at = fields.LocalDateTime(
        format=FOO_TIME_FORMAT,
        default=lambda: str(datetime(2017, 5, 4, 3, 2, 1)), 
        missing=lambda: str(datetime(2017, 9, 8, 7, 6, 5)),
        required=False, allow_none=False
    )
    

schema = FooSchema()

json_str, dumps_errors = schema.dumps(Foo())
json_str
# => '{"created_at": null}'
data, loads_errors = schema.loads('{}')
data
# => {}
~~~

**Tested on `Python` and `marshmallow` versions:**

~~~python
import marshmallow
import sys
sys.version
=> '2.7.12+ (default, Sep 17 2016, 12:08:02) \n[GCC 6.2.0 20160914]'
marshmallow.__version__
=> '2.13.0'
~~~

and

~~~python
import marshmallow
import sys
sys.version
'3.5.2+ (default, Sep 22 2016, 12:18:14) \n[GCC 6.2.0 20160927]'
marshmallow.__version__
=> '2.13.0'
~~~

issue_title: Style transfer: make coreml model to have flexible shape input/output
issue_body:
First, it's not clear from the docstring whether it is (width, height) or (height, width). Internally, we often use (height, width) for implementation details. However, in for instance `tc.image_analysis.resize` the `width` argument precedes the `height` argument. So, we should first think about which is the most appropriate from a user point of view and then make this clear in the docstring.

We could also try to avoid this issue by separating into separate `width` and `height` parameters.

issue_title: Location.get_clearsky, irradiance.extraradiation, and solarposition.ephemeris failures in pandas 0.20
issue_body:
The ``DatetimeIndex`` attribute behavior changed in 0.20, and this leads to problems in the ``Location.get_clearsky`` ``dni_extra`` calculation and thus the clearsky calculation.

```python
        if dni_extra is None:
            dni_extra = irradiance.extraradiation(times.dayofyear)
```

pandas 0.19.2

```python
In [2]: times = pd.date_range(start='20140624', freq='6H', periods=4, tz='US/Arizona')

In [3]: times.dayofyear
Out[3]: array([175, 175, 175, 175], dtype=int32)

In [5]: pvlib.irradiance.extraradiation(times.dayofyear)
Out[5]: array([ 1321.16558348,  1321.16558348,  1321.16558348,  1321.16558348])
```

pandas 0.20.1

```python
In [2]: times = pd.date_range(start='20140624', freq='6H', periods=4, tz='US/Arizona')

In [3]: times.dayofyear
Out[3]: Int64Index([175, 175, 175, 175], dtype='int64')

In [5]: pvlib.irradiance.extraradiation(times.dayofyear)
Out[5]: Float64Index([1321.16558348, 1321.16558348, 1321.16558348, 1321.16558348], dtype='float64')
```

Simply passing ``times`` (no ``.dayofyear``) will return a ``Series``, and everything works again. I don't know why I chose to use ``times.dayofyear`` instead of just ``times`` when building this method. Maybe ``irradiance.extraradiation`` didn't accept Series back then?

Next, the ``solarposition.ephemeris`` function contains

```python
    DayOfYear = time_utc.dayofyear
    DecHours = (time_utc.hour + time_utc.minute/60. + time_utc.second/3600. +
                time_utc.microsecond/3600.e6)
```

Wrapping these variables with calls to ``np.array`` should ensure they're the right type.

I'll make a PR soon.

issue_title: Triggering lemma learning to reduce quantifier instantiations in incremental mode
issue_body:
Heads up: this is basically a feature request, but for a feature that [we](http://viper.ethz.ch) expect to be useful for many users and that we hope won't be too difficult to implement.

A bit of context: we use Z3 as the back-end of a symbolic-execution-based program verifier, and while exploring a program path we push more and more assumptions to Z3 and we repeatedly check if certain conditions are guaranteed to hold. I.e. we use Z3 in incremental mode with lots of (nested) push-pop blocks and we typically get the following program structure:

```
; Verification preamble:
;   - declaring sorts and functions
;   - axiomatising theories via quantifiers
;   - ...

(push)
  (assert ...)
  ...
  (push)
    (assert ...)
    ...
    (push)
      (assert (not P))
      (check-sat) ;;; unsat --> P holds
    (pop)
    ...
    (push)
      (assert (not Q))
      (check-sat) ; unsat --> Q holds
    (pop)
    ...
  (pop)
(pop)
```

As is "common knowledge", the first property checks (`P`) causes Z3 to learn lots of lemmas, e.g. by instantiating quantifiers (we don't use MBQI), but these lemmas are forgotten upon pop and thus need to be relearned during the second property check (`Q`).

However, lots of the learned lemmas are independent of the checked properties, e.g. they could already be learned right after the verification preamble. Indeed, some lemmas appear to be learned upon first `push`: our understanding is that Z3 performs some kind of cursory lemma learning whenever a new push-pop block is entered.  
Adding a `check-sat` *right after* (but curiously, not *before*) the first `push` seems to make Z3 learn even more lemmas — this seems to be an easy way to reduce the number of lemmas Z3 will have to learn again and again.

Attached SMT file [state_saturation.smt2.txt](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/files/1156328/state_saturation.smt2.txt) — the interesting (and short) bit starts on line 919 — illustrates the above, including the positive effect of an initial `check-sat`. The file is a cut-down version of an example generated by our symbolic execution engine; for the original file, the initial `check-sat` reduces Z3's runtime from 14 to 3 seconds.

However, it is unclear to us how "hard" Z3 tries to learn lemmas upon such additional `check-sat`s. After all, Z3 could probably be lucky and conclude `sat` right away, or it could give up immediately and conclude `unknown`, without having learned many lemmas.

It might thus be helpful to have a command that forces Z3 to learn as many lemmas as possible (modulo quantifier triggering, etc.) in a given amount of time (i.e. until a user-provided timeout is reached). 

Does all of this sound reasonable? Do you think such a command would be useful?

issue_title: datatype declarations according to SMT v2.6 not possible
issue_body:
Datatype declarations such as 

```
(declare-datatype MyList (par (T) ((nelem) (cons (hd T) (tl (MyList T)))))) 
```

are not supported by z3 (version 4.5.1). When executed with this input, z3 will respond with the following message:

```
unsupported
; declare-datatype line: 1 position: 75

```

For more information please have a look at this [post](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/47620234/modelling-generic-datatypes-for-z3-and-or-smtv2-6/47623885#comment82276649_47623885).

```python
import numpy as np
import theano
import theano.tensor as T
import lasagne

batch_size = 1

data = np.random.rand(10, 3, 425, 620).astype(np.float32)

net = lasagne.layers.InputLayer(input_var=input_var, shape=(batch_size, ) + data.shape[1:])

out_var = lasagne.layers.get_output(net)

conv_fun = theano.function([input_var], outputs=out_var)

for id_batch in xrange(0, data.shape[0], batch_size):

    filtered_array[id_batch:id_batch + batch_size] = conv_fun(data[id_batch:id_batch + batch_size])

issue_title: RQAlpha 当前 Universe 在 tick 级别策略中的逻辑 bug。
issue_body:
RQAlpha 当前 tick 逻辑如下：

> 策略 Universe 中的合约会触发 tick 事件，tick 事件一方面触发合约最新价格变化，另一方面触发 handle_tick。

在此逻辑下，若策略的持仓中有未订阅的合约，则该合约对应持仓的 holding_pnl，total_value 等不会动态更新，导致策略持仓、账户等计算错误。

可行的解决办法：
1.  将 Universe 拆分，在 rqalpha 层和策略层维护两个 universe，rqalpha 层的 universe 是用户 universe 以及持仓合约的并集。该方案不会改变现有 universe 的概念和逻辑，但会影响性能，且比较丑陋。
2. 更改 universe 逻辑，发生交易时自动帮策略订阅合约，且策略不可取消订阅当前持仓的合约。该方案可能会导致老策略的运行结果与之前相比发生变化，但逻辑上比较合理。

issue_title: Error when loading completion from zsh
issue_body:
Under zsh, [you run the following code](https://github.com/click-contrib/click-completion/blob/master/click_completion/zsh.j2):

```bash
if [[ "$(basename ${(%):-%x})" != "..." ]]; then
```

Under a login shell, the shell executable begins with a dash (eg. '-zsh'), so `basename` fails with:

```bash
$ eval "$(pipenv --completion)"
basename: illegal option -- z
usage: basename string [suffix]
       basename [-a] [-s suffix] string [...]
```

(`pipenv` uses `click_completion` internally.) To fix this, you can do:

```bash
basename -- ${(%):-%x}
```

Adding `--` stops anything starting with `-` from being parsed as an argument.

Rob

issue_title: collection_timestamp() always fails when Kinto is readonly
issue_body:
Regression from [7.5.1 to 8.1.3](https://github.com/Kinto/kinto/compare/7.5.1...8.1.3)

In the past, we were only attempting to insert into the timestamp table when everything was empty. We could have an error when accessing the collection via a readonly instance on an empty table. But accessing via a writable instance was enough, since it would create the row and never try again. 

It seems that in the last version, it always try to create (UPSERT?) even if it's not empty. It thus always fails to execute collection_timestamp() on a readonly instance.

issue_title: Why should uvcvideo be a module (rather than a built-in)?
issue_body:
My question is not specific to a sensor, but rather the library itself (thus I'm not including the device/model tags at the top of my question).

I noticed that the latest SDK release for NVIDIA Jetson TX1/TX2 boards (which was released only a few days ago) uses kernel v4.4 and the sources already contain the necessary patches for RealSense video formats, etc. 

Right out of the box, I noticed that upon connecting my R200 to my Jetson TX1 board, `dmesg` indicated that the connected camera was correctly recognized: 

> Found UVC 1.10 device Intel RealSense 3D Camera R200

I could also confirm that `uvcvideo` was already available as a builtin. I proceeded with building the latest librealsense source. However, I noticed that upon trying to run any of the samples I would get:

> uvcvideo kernel module is not loaded

I went ahead and built the kernel from the sources, from scratch. Except for forcing `uvcvideo` to be built as a module, I did not touch any of the other settings. Now the camera and the samples work fine without any issues.

Forgive my ignorance (in case this is a stupid question) but is there a reason that `uvcvideo` should be built as a module for librealsense to work?

### Issue Description
<Describe your issue / question / feature request / etc..>

1. There are multiple HSE band structure workflows:

* GGA band structure + HSE gap correction
* GGA geometry + HSE band structure
* HSE geometry + HSE band structure

These are not documented or distinguished very well in the code.

2. Furthermore, when changing functionals like HSE, should task names change - e.g., to communicate to the builders that this is a different type of run? If so, how can we keep this systematic?

It's possible that 1 and 2 should be separate issues. Someone can feel free to separate them out if they've solved half of this issue but not the other half.

I think there are a few issues with the RDF calculation method ([link for convenience](https://github.com/hackingmaterials/matminer/blob/master/matminer/descriptors/structure_features.py#L40))

1. RDF are typically normalized by number density. This function appears normalize by mass density
2. In the loop over pairs of neighbors, pair distances are used as keys for the `dist_rdf` dict. In the loop when normalizing `dist_rdf`, it seems like the keys are treated as bin indices (based on the shell thickness calculation)

If you agree these are problems, I can make these changes. As you have tests for this function already, I figured it worth checking in before altering both the code and the tests.

better tutorials (e.g. show off featurize_dataframe, better use of featurizers, include structure featurizers, separate into “getting data”, “featurizing data”, and “full data mining workflow”, make sure they work with Py3 (no print ‘x’), use built-in example datasets for tutorials, etc.

issue_title: BatchingBolt which is volume-based, not time-based.
issue_body:
_From @rduplain on September 1, 2015 6:0_

The current BatchingBolt implementation is based on the number of ticks. A variant could accumulate tuples (or even values contained in those tuples) until a certain threshold is hit (e.g. every 100 tuples). This would be useful when e.g. batching to an upstream/downstream service that is rate-limited, as to send a full request.

This would likely need some time-delay featured in which the threshold is ignored after a certain wait, as to avoid holding onto values indefinitely if the threshold is never hit.

_Copied from original issue: Parsely/streamparse#182_

issue_title: Binary Coded Decimal Values Do Not Get Decoded
issue_body:
## Issue summary

When adding a new game that uses binary coded decimal (four bytes) to store its score, I
added this to the appropriate data.json:

```json
{                                  
  "info": { 
    "lives": {
        "address": 16722333,
        "type": "|u1"
    },

    },                             
    "wave": {                      
        "address": 16722414,       
        "type": "|u1"              
    }                              
  }                                
}                                  
```
But when I do an env.step(action), I end up with:

```python
{'lives': 3, 'score': 5456, 'wave': 1}   # The info dict returned from step()

issue_title: wrong panid used in discovery request.
issue_body:
It looks like the factoryreset, resets the radio (via otPlatReset) but does not reset the panid in the openthread stack.
According to the documentation the factoryreset will “Delete all stored settings, and signal a platform reset.”
Shouldn’t the panid in the openthread stack then also not be set to 0xffff ?

This can result in the wrong panId is used during Joining.
This can be seen during e.g. during certification test 8.1.1
In my test, the DUT is controlled by the official Thread test harness (via the THCI).
The test harness performs several settings on the DUT before the test.
The settings before the joining starts are:
1) (… many other settings…)
2) factoryreset
3) commissioner provisioningurl
4) extaddr 166e0a0000000001
5) channel 20
6) dataset channel 20
7) ifconfig up
8) joiner start threadjpaketest
… at this point the discovery starts.

As you can see in the settings above, no new panId was set _AFTER_ the factoryreset (note: it is set several times to 0xface _BEFORE_ the factoryreset).

But in the sniffer log the discovery request is sent with destination panid 0xface, and no source panid. (Therefor the source panid is also 0xface)
However, given the factoryreset, the source and destination panids should have been 0xffff.

issue_title: Include local Dataset's Timestamp TLV in Child ID Request message after reset
issue_body:
This is the last issue to pass Certification test 9.2.15 and 9.2.16([Router_9_2_15.zip](https://github.com/openthread/openthread/files/566649/Router_9_2_15.zip)), the test steps:
1. DUT attaches network and store Active Dataset and Pending Dataset locally;
2. DUT power down for a moment, some changes will happen to the network;
3. DUT power on and try to reattach to network, Harness checks the present of original Active/Pending Timestamp TLVs in Child ID Request message, we failed with this check.

But I think it 's better to **not** include local stored Dataset's Timestamp TLV in Child ID Request message, since after the reset (assume no changes happened to the network), the Delay Timer should be out of date, and we need to get the refreshed Pending Dataset with updated Delay Time from Child ID Response message. But if we include the local Timestamp TLVs in Child ID Request message as Harness expected, the parent will not include Datasets in Child ID Response. @jwhui , May I know your opinion on this issue? Thanks.

However, we could still make some changes to satisfy Harness check and pass the tests:
Include the local Dataset's Timestamp TLV in Child ID Request message, and enable local Datasets as network Datasets if parent do not indicate that it has a different Dataset.

issue_title: One Hot encoding defaults to on_value=1.0 AND off_value=1.0
issue_body:
Hi.

I think I may have found a typo: The `off_value` parameter of the `one_hot_encoding` function in `core.py` ([here](https://github.com/tflearn/tflearn/blob/7b13632ac1e781548f19f201e9eb98dc5e07d7b0/tflearn/layers/core.py#L544
)) defaults to `1.0`. Tensorflow itself defaults to a 0 here, and if my understanding of One Hot Encoding is correct, tflearn really should default to 0 as well.


Let me know if you want a pull request, or if this is so trivial to fix that that is not necessary. If it isn't a simple typo, then the reasoning behind it should probably be documented.

issue_body:
Hi,
this is not directly an issue with vdirsyncer, but rather with this repository: All the documentation links, link to the "/stable/" tag on ReadTheDocs, which is from February (Release 0.15). A lot of fixes in the documentation happend since then e.g. regarding the problem with quoted strings in the configuration file. Therefore it would maybe suitable to use the "/latest/" links from RTD to include all of these fixes.

Best regards,
    Felix

As demonstrated in #416 and mentioned in #418, the current way of mapping collections is unflexible. In the most common usecase (`DAV <=> filesystem`) this only means that collection's folder names are determined by the server, which can be easily worked around with symlinks, but when syncing server-to-server, you might be unable to correctly map already existing collections against each other.

This is related to #405, but not the same issue.

There are multiple ways to solve this:

* Have a interactive UI to connect collections from each side with each other when running `vdirsyncer discover`. A primitive implementation of this is #418.
* Somehow extend the configuration format for this. Unsure how this would look like.
* Simply match by displayname (easiest). This requires:
  * That the user has unique displaynames set for each calendar per-side (this should be rigorously checked)
  * That all servers support displaynames
  * That every collection has a non-empty displayname

issue_title: Wrong arg pass in systemd: certbot-renew.service can't work
issue_body:
## My operating system is (include version):
Centos 7.3

## I installed Certbot with (certbot-auto, OS package manager, pip, etc):
EPEL package certbot.noarch 0.12.0-4.el7

## I ran this command and it produced this output:
systemctl start certbot-renew.service

## Certbot's behavior differed from what I expected because:
File `/lib/systemd/system/certbot-renew.service` from package has lines
```
[Service]
EnvironmentFile=-/etc/sysconfig/certbot
Type=oneshot
ExecStart=/usr/bin/certbot renew --pre-hook $PRE_HOOK --post-hook $POST_HOOK --renew-hook $RENEW_HOOK $CERTBOT_ARGS
```
Therefore, it rely on `/etc/sysconfig/certbot` to set the variables. Ones are set by default like 
```
PRE_HOOK="''"
```
So, something goes wrong with variables passing, systemd clears all type of quotes and puts nothing instead of ''

If I simulate certbot run with command from shell prompt:
```
/usr/bin/certbot renew --pre-hook '' --post-hook '' --renew-hook ''
```
It is Ok, it works.

The workaround is to put any "do nothing" command in the quotes, for example:
```
PRE_HOOK="'echo >/dev/null'"
```
And the same to all others. Now it works.

## Here is a Certbot log showing the issue (if available):
No cerbot logs created at all.

System messages log shows:
```
May 17 00:00:06 pavlyuts systemd: Starting This service automatically renews any certbot certificates found...
May 17 00:00:06 pavlyuts certbot: usage:
May 17 00:00:06 pavlyuts certbot: certbot [SUBCOMMAND] [options] [-d DOMAIN] [-d DOMAIN] ...

May 17 00:00:06 pavlyuts certbot: certbot: error: argument --pre-hook: expected one argument
May 17 00:00:07 pavlyuts systemd: certbot-renew.service: main process exited, code=exited, status=2/INVALIDARGUMENT

issue_title: Ask if you want to share your e-mail address with EFF

issue_body:
I haven't done any measurements myself, but I've been told it takes Certbot 10 - 15 seconds to obtain a certificate and was asked if we can do this more in the <5 second range. This would be particularly useful in large deployments using Certbot.

I suspect this won't be very hard to do because we've spent almost no time trying to optimize Certbot.

The one exception to this might be the Apache and Nginx plugins where we sleep for 3 seconds after setting up the DV challenge. The reason for this is the command you use to ask both webservers to reload their config simply sends a signal to the webserver and immediately returns. This command returning does not mean the server has finished loading their config though and I'm unaware of a reliable way for us to know when this process is complete. As a result, we currently just sleep for a few seconds and afterwards assume the config has been reloaded.

issue_title: Webroot plugin should delete .well-known/acme-challenge on cleanup
issue_body: `cleanup()` should delete `.well-known/acme-challenge` when all previously perfomed challenges have been cleaned up. This requires us to track the status of all incoming challenges, just like standalone2 does.

issue_title: How to handle non-admin polymorphic forms?
issue_body:
Let say I have an Employee model like this:

```
class Employee(models.Model):
    employee_id = models.CharField(...)
    company = models.ForeignKey(Company, ...)
```

and I'd like to attach a polymorphic address (to handle country-specific addressing rules). I create a "base" polymorphic class which contains a read-only "jurisdiction" like this:

```
class PostalDetail(PolymorphicModel):
    jurisdiction = models.CharField(max_length=ISO3166_MAX_LENGTH, editable=False)
    employee = models.ForeignKey(Employee, on_delete=models.CASCADE)
    line1 = models.CharField(max_length=LINE_MAX_LENGTH, help_text=_("First line of address"))
    ...
```

I can now define subclasses of PostalDetail to cover GB, US, whatever. Depending on which subclass it is, I can programmatically fill in the jurisdiction (based, as it happens, on the subclass model name). Now, using the admin facilities provided by django-polymorphic, I can create an admin interface based on PostalDetail that will let me create records of GB or US-specific flavours.

However, I'm left wondering how to create non-admin forms, like normal Django model forms, to create these addresses. I can see the formset support, but some guidance/exmaples/hints on how to proceed without formsets would be welcome.

I just submitted a patch to enable daemonized tasks and the default `--no-wait` behavior. It has a strong impact on the use of tasks so everyone should be aware of the change. It might also have introduced some weird behavior and bugs.

The changes are
1. daemonize tasks so that they are executed independent of sos. This should have been done long time ago, but there has been some technical difficulties (conflict between multiprocessing and `os.fork`).
2. when `sos` is waiting for the execution of tasks and there is no runnable node, sos would quit without waiting for the completion of tasks.
3. Option `-w` can be added to change the behavior to `wait`.

More testing is needed but it appears that
1. running in `wait` mode (option `-w`) + `Ctrl-C` would kill the tasks. This should be fixed if this is the case.

issue_title: Remove completed tasks from Jupyer notebook
issue_body:
There are two problems with the existing workflow

1. the completed job list can be long  but does not provide useful information.
2. the status of the tasks is undefined when a notebook is opened.

We should then remove completed tasks from jupyter notebook, during the last "refresh" call to the cell.
After trying to start a task and I have not been able to connect it again.

Julia is likely the next language to be supported, because

1. It is well supported by Jupyter, and is one of the "standard" kernels of Jupyter
2. It is part of the Jupyter notebook docker image on which `sos-notebook` docker image is based
3. It is trendy for data scientists
4. iJulia is immensely popular among Julia users so supporting Julia could attract them to SoS

Note that
* [introduction to Julia types](https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/Introducing_Julia/Types)
* [Julia reads feather format](https://github.com/JuliaStats/Feather.jl) so it is easy to transfer Python dataframe to Julia.

issue_title: Factory with a self-referential foreign key is not saving to the database.
issue_body:
I'm trying to create a fixture (Django 1.11) based on a model with a self-referential foreign key.
Ok, there is no error, Channel is saved, but Categories not.

My model.py:

```python
    class Channel(models.Model):
        name = models.CharField(max_length=100)
        def __str__(self):
            return self.name
    
    class Category(models.Model):
        name = models.CharField(max_length=100)
        channel = models.ForeignKey(Channel)
        parent = models.ForeignKey('Category', null=True)
        def __str__(self):
            return self.name
```

My facctories.py

```python  
    class ChannelFactory(factory.django.DjangoModelFactory):
        class Meta:
            model = Channel
 
     class CategoryFactory(factory.django.DjangoModelFactory):
        class Meta:
            model = Category
    
        channel_id=1
        parent = factory.SubFactory('core.factories.CategoryFactory')
```

issue_title: Email validation, gives an error: the custom function requires 3 arguments 4 given..
issue_body:
When creating a SOCIAL_AUTH_EMAIL_VALIDATION_FUNCTION, the documentation says that it ["should take three arguments strategy, backend and code"](http://python-social-auth.readthedocs.io/en/latest/pipeline.html#email-validation). The example project also creates a function with 3 parameters.  But this gives an error that says that the custom functions requires 3 params and 4 given. 


Upon debugging I found that line 133 in the _send_email_validation()_ in strategy.py sends 4 params when calling the custom function. 

```
    def send_email_validation(self, backend, email, partial_token=None):
        email_validation = self.setting('EMAIL_VALIDATION_FUNCTION')
        send_email = module_member(email_validation)
        code = self.storage.code.make_code(email)
        send_email(self, backend, code, partial_token)
        return code
```


removing the **_partial_token_**, hides the error. 



**Is this a valid fix, or am I doing something wrong**, if yes on the former, what should I do to contribute...:)


social-auth-app-django version 2.1.0
social-auth-core version 1.6.0

issue_title: [rllib] Change RL agent classes to take function that creates env.
issue_body:
Currently the RL agent constructions take in a string with the name of the environment to train in, e.g.,

```python
env_name = 'Pong-v0'
agent = PPOAgent(env_name, DEFAULT_CONFIG)
```

The agent constructor then creates the environment by calling `gym.make('Pong-v0')`.

Following the discussion in https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/ray-dev/r4YrZXdCfw0, we should probably modify the API to be the following.

```python
def env_creator():
    return gym.make('Pong-v0')

agent = PPOAgent(env_creator, DEFAULT_CONFIG)
```

- `ray.put` and `ray.get` seem to do nothing, instead of creating fake object IDs or serializing/unserializing. It would be useful for them to emulate the object store, in terms of debugging issues with serialization and also with missing calls to `ray.get` or `ray.put`.
- If a remote function which raises an exception is called by the driver, then the exception is not deferred until `ray.get` is called.

With `PYTHON_MODE`:
```
>>> import ray
>>> ray.init(driver_mode=ray.PYTHON_MODE)
{'redis_address': None, 'node_ip_address': None}
>>> ray.get(ray.put(0))
0
>>> ray.get(0)
0
>>> @ray.remote
... def raises():
...     raise Exception
...
>>> raises.remote()
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File ".../ray/worker.py", line 2312, in func_call
    result = func(*copy.deepcopy(args))
  File "<stdin>", line 3, in raises
Exception
```

Without `PYTHON_MODE`:
```
>>> import ray
>>> ray.init()
>>> ray.get(ray.put(0))
0L
>>> ray.get(0)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File ".../ray/worker.py", line 2053, in get
    value = worker.get_object([object_ids])[0]
  File "...ray/worker.py", line 394, in get_object
    "which is not an ObjectID.".format(object_id))
Exception: Attempting to call `get` on the value 0, which is not an ObjectID.
>>> @ray.remote
... def raises():
...     raise Exception
...
>>> raises.remote()
ObjectID(df68230187eb62dc5b50f359d7f648fb25d86045)
```

issue_title: Brainstorming schema to use for visualization dependency graph of tasks and objects.
issue_body:
Below is example code for generating json descriptions of the dependency graph that can be used to produce a visualization.

Script for generating the data.

Light: `M = N = P = 10`
Heavy: `M = N = P = 100`
Heavier: `M = N = P = 1000`

```python
import numpy as np
import ray
import time

M = 100
N = 100
P = 100

ray.init()

@ray.remote
def load_data(i):
  return np.random.normal(size=10000)

@ray.remote
def aggregate(x, y):
  return x + y

@ray.remote
def simple_function(x):
  time.sleep(np.random.uniform(0.1, 0.2))
  return x

data = [load_data.remote(i) for i in range(M)]
while len(data) > 1:
  data.append(aggregate.remote(data.pop(0), data.pop(0)))
results = ray.get(data)

previous = simple_function
cumulative = 0
for _ in range(N):
